turns.en: At this altitude, I can run flat out for a half mile before my hands start shaking. Can I ask? I'm a cybernetic organism, living tissues. Hello boys and girls, ladies and germs. This is Tim Ferriss, welcome to another episode of The Tim Ferriss Show, where it is always my job to interview and deconstruct world-class performers from all different fields. My guest today is a dear friend. He does very few podcasts. We're going to get into a disclaimer around that. Naval Ravikant. You can find him at @naval on Twitter. He is the co-founder and chairman of AngelList. He's an angel investor and has invested in more than 200 companies, including many mega successes such as Twitter, Uber, Notion, OpenDoor, Postmates, and Wish. It is a long, long list. You can subscribe to Naval's podcast on wealth and happiness on Apple Podcast, Spotify, or wherever you get your podcasts. You can also find his blog at nav.al for even more Naval. Plus Tim—that's me—check out my wildly popular interview with him from 2015, which was nominated for Podcast of the Year. You can find that and resources and much more at tim.blog/naval. Naval, welcome back to the show.
turns.zh: 在这个高度，我可以全速跑半英里，然后手才开始抖。我能问一下吗？我是一个电子有机体，活体组织。大家好，男孩女孩们，女士们，细菌们。我是蒂姆·费里斯，欢迎收听另一期《蒂姆·费里斯秀》，我的工作始终是采访和解析来自不同领域的顶尖表现者。我今天的嘉宾是一位亲爱的朋友。他很少做播客。我们稍后会对此做一个免责声明。纳瓦尔·拉维康特。你可以在 Twitter 上找到他，账号是 @naval。他是 AngelList 的联合创始人兼董事长。他是一位天使投资人，投资了 200 多家公司，包括许多超级成功的公司，如 Twitter、Uber、Notion、OpenDoor、Postmates 和 Wish。名单很长很长。你可以在 Apple Podcast、Spotify 或任何你收听播客的地方订阅纳瓦尔关于财富和幸福的播客。你也可以在他的博客 nav.al 上找到更多纳瓦尔的内容。还有蒂姆——也就是我——去看看我在 2015 年对他做的非常受欢迎的采访，那次采访被提名了年度播客。你可以在 tim.blog/naval 找到那期节目以及资源和更多内容。纳瓦尔，欢迎回到节目。

turns.en: Yeah, thanks for having me. If I recall correctly, I was crushed in Podcast of the Year by Jamie Foxx. So that's right, I need weaker competition this time around. If you could just tone it down this year, please. I will do my best.
turns.zh: 嗯，谢谢邀请。如果我没记错的话，我在年度播客评选中被杰米·福克斯碾压了。没错，这次我需要弱一点的对手。如果你今年能低调一点，拜托了。我会尽力的。

turns.en: Yeah, Jamie basically—I could have been a completely non-responsive critical care patient as long as the equipment functioned. Jamie Foxx is the consummate performer, so he is formidable competition in podcast. But you are a different breed, and I understand that you get asked to do podcasts a lot. So what type of disclaimer would you like?
turns.zh: 是啊，杰米基本上——只要设备还能运转，我完全可以是一个毫无反应的重症监护病人。杰米·福克斯是完美的表演者，所以他在播客界是强大的对手。但你是另一种类型，我知道你经常被邀请做播客。那么你想要什么样的免责声明呢？

turns.en: Yeah, I don't go on other people's podcasts anymore. I had to make an exception for you because you're the OG. You put me on the map. You're a good friend, and frankly, I wouldn't even be known if it weren't for you. So I definitely have to thank you and honor your request to have me back. I generally don't do podcasts anymore, or at least other people's podcasts, for a couple of reasons. First is, I just don't believe in sequels. I think sequels suck.
turns.zh: 嗯，我不再上别人的播客了。我必须为你破例，因为你是 OG（元老）。你让我出名了。你是个好朋友，坦率地说，如果不是你，我根本不会被人知道。所以我必须感谢你，并满足你让我回来的请求。我通常不再做播客了，或者至少不做别人的播客，有几个原因。首先，我不相信续集。我觉得续集都很烂。

ts.sec: On Sequels and Starting the Conversation

turns.en: Right, you look at sequels in movies and whatnot, and they're usually pretty bad. They cost three times as much, they're long-winded, the person now has an ego and a reputation to defend. They've already said what they were going to say, so they're just repeating themselves to some level. Maybe the first run they took an idea they've had for a decade or three decades and expounded on it, and it sounded great. Then in the sequel, whether it's a book or a movie, they have to come up with something equally good but only have two years to do it. It's just really difficult. So I think sequels just make everybody look bad, and I'm not a fan of sequels. Yet here we are. The thing about sequels: they make money, people want them, people crave them because there's a certain familiarity. But hopefully we won't embarrass ourselves too much. So yes, I don't do sequels. We'll make an exception here and kind of see how it goes. Just don't have Jamie Foxx back on, at least not this year.
turns.zh: 你看电影续集之类的，通常都很糟糕。成本翻三倍，冗长拖沓，创作者有了自负和名声要维护。他们已经把想说的说完了，所以在某种程度上只是重复自己。也许第一部时，他们把一个酝酿了十年或三十年的想法阐述出来，听起来很棒。但到了续集，无论是书还是电影，他们必须拿出同样好的东西，却只有两年时间。这真的很难。所以我认为续集只会让所有人难堪，我不喜欢续集。但我们还是来了。续集的好处是：赚钱，人们想要，人们渴望，因为有一种熟悉感。但希望我们不会太丢脸。是的，我不做续集。这次破个例，看看效果如何。只是别让杰米·福克斯再来了，至少今年别。

turns.en: Well, I am looking right now, as a framework for starting this conversation, at your Twitter account. That might seem like an odd place for me to start. People who listen to this show a lot will know that I don't often do that. But I want to know two things: you follow zero people on Twitter, yet you have almost a million followers. Also, trending right now in the United States is Jamie Foxx hilarious. He's right next to you, he's reminding me this cannot keep this guy down. So let's talk about your account. I find your account fascinating, I find your use of Twitter fascinating, and I'd like to begin with a photograph of a professor at a blackboard that you have as your profile header. Who is the person in the photograph? I think I recognize this person, but I want to hear you describe why you chose this photograph and who it is.
turns.zh: 嗯，我现在看着你的推特账号，作为开启对话的框架。这对我来说可能是个奇怪的起点。经常听这个节目的人会知道我不常这么做。但我想知道两件事：你在推特上关注了零个人，却有近百万粉丝。还有，美国现在 trending 的是杰米·福克斯 hilarious。他就在你旁边，他提醒我这个人压不住。所以让我们谈谈你的账号。我觉得你的账号很迷人，你对推特的使用很迷人，我想从你个人资料头图里那张教授在黑板的照片开始。照片里的人是谁？我想我认出了这个人，但我想听你描述为什么选这张照片，以及他是谁。

turns.en: Yeah, that's Professor Richard Feynman. He was a pioneer in the field of quantum electrodynamics, part of the Manhattan Project. He's known colloquially for his book 'Surely You're Joking, Mr. Feynman!' and a couple of related ones like 'What Do You Care What Other People Think?' But he's also very well regarded in the field of physics. He passed away a while back, but he invented a lot of quantum electrodynamics, Feynman path integrals. He was part of the Manhattan Project. Brilliant, brilliant guy. I loved him because Feynman was one of the first characters I encountered that did science and serious work and was accomplished in so-called real life, but was also a character. He was a happy person, deeply philosophical, didn't take himself nor life too seriously. He appreciated the mysteries of life, appreciated living life, and had a lot of fun along the way. To me, he was like a full-stack intellectual hacker of life, and was very inspirational to me as a kid growing up. Even though I read a lot of philosophy and normal non-fiction, my hero—and even though I'm in the business world, the tech world—my heroes are scientists. I think applied science is the engine that pulls humanity forward. It eventually becomes technology, and technology allows us to engage in all kinds of pursuits around civilization rather than scrapping for basic subsistence and survival. So I think scientists are still the most unsung heroes of human history, and Feynman was one of the greats. He was admirable in many regards as to how he lived his life, although I think recently they tried to cancel him, but they try to cancel everybody.
turns.zh: 是的，那是理查德·费曼教授。他是量子电动力学领域的先驱，参与了曼哈顿计划。他因《别闹了，费曼先生！》等书而广为人知。他在物理学界备受尊敬。他去世有一阵子了，但他发明了很多量子电动力学，费曼路径积分。他参与了曼哈顿计划。非常非常聪明的人。我喜欢他，因为费曼是我遇到的第一个既做科学和严肃工作、在所谓现实生活中有所成就，同时又是个有趣角色的人。他是个快乐的人，深具哲学思想，不把自己或生活太当回事。他欣赏生活的奥秘，享受生活，一路上乐趣无穷。对我来说，他就像一个全栈的知识黑客，对我小时候的成长非常有启发。尽管我读了很多哲学和普通非虚构作品，我的英雄——尽管我在商界、科技界——我的英雄是科学家。我认为应用科学是推动人类前进的引擎。它最终变成技术，技术让我们能够从事文明的各种追求，而不是为基本生存而挣扎。所以我认为科学家仍然是人类历史上最被低估的英雄，费曼是伟人之一。他在生活方式上有很多值得钦佩的地方，尽管最近有人试图取消他，但他们试图取消所有人。

turns.en: Feynman is a character, certainly. He is a figure who has fascinated me for decades. Actually, I collect very few things, but managed to buy some of his papers and drawings at auction a handful of years ago because it was the first time they had been made available. I can show you some of his equations from the Manhattan Project, which is wild to think about. My fascination, though, is in many respects uninformed because I don't have the scientific literacy to fully appreciate the genius he brought to bear on physics. But I could appreciate his cleverness, his questioning of assumptions, and also his distaste for uniforms and other accolades, most notably some of the prizes he was awarded that he seemed to have a particular dislike for. He famously said, 'Science is the belief in the ignorance of experts.' It's so funny because these days so many people on Twitter are saying 'believe in science' or 'according to science,' and you have to realize there's no such thing as science with a capital S. The foundation of science is doubt; it is all about falsifiability. David Deutsch, who is one of my current modern living physicist heroes, is a Popperian—he subscribes to Karl Popper's philosophy. He basically says that if it's not falsifiable, it's not scientific. If you can't prove it false, if it's true up or down or left or right, it's not science. It doesn't require belief. You should be able to challenge it at all times. It should make risky predictions that are not obvious, and those predictions should then be tested. You shouldn't be able to after the fact change the goal, move the goalpost, or change how the predictions were set up. Those are the hallmarks of good science: falsifiability, independent verifiability, and making risky and narrow predictions with details that are hard to vary both before and after the fact. That's how you end up with good science and good explanations, not by people on Twitter voting. That's politics. Like everything has become politics now. Science has become politics too. People are politicized, and there's big science. But I think Feynman would be very unhappy with that. He was just a generally happy character and non-political, intended not to get involved in these things. But I think he would realize that that is not actually science, but it's masquerading as science. It's almost becoming social science. And going to Twitter, like Nassim Taleb is one of my favorite Twitter followers even though he's a bit cantankerous.
turns.zh: 费曼是个角色，当然。他是一个让我着迷了几十年的人物。实际上，我很少收藏东西，但几年前在拍卖会上买了他的一些论文和画作，因为那是它们首次公开。我可以给你看一些他在曼哈顿计划中的方程，想想就觉得疯狂。不过，我的迷恋在很多方面是信息不足的，因为我没有足够的科学素养来充分欣赏他在物理学上的天才。但我能欣赏他的聪明、他对假设的质疑，以及他对制服和其他荣誉的厌恶，尤其是他获得的一些奖项，他似乎特别不喜欢。他有一句名言：'科学是对专家无知的信仰。' 这很有趣，因为现在很多人在推特上说'相信科学'或'根据科学'，你必须意识到没有大写的科学这种东西。科学的基础是怀疑；它完全关乎可证伪性。大卫·多伊奇是我目前现代在世物理学家中的英雄之一，他是波普尔主义者——他信奉卡尔·波普尔的哲学。他基本上说，如果不可证伪，就不是科学。如果你无法证明它是假的，如果它无论上下左右都是真的，那就不是科学。它不需要信仰。你应该随时能够挑战它。它应该做出不明显的、有风险的预测，然后这些预测应该被检验。你不能事后改变目标、移动球门柱，或改变预测的设置方式。这些是优秀科学的标志：可证伪性、独立可验证性，以及做出有风险且狭窄的预测，细节在事前事后都难以改变。这样你才能得到好的科学和好的解释，而不是靠推特上的人投票。那是政治。就像现在一切都变成了政治。科学也变成了政治。人们被政治化，有大科学。但我认为费曼会对此非常不满。他通常是个快乐的角色，非政治化，有意不卷入这些事情。但我想他会意识到那实际上不是科学，而是伪装成科学。它几乎变成了社会科学。至于推特，纳西姆·塔勒布是我最喜欢的推特关注者之一，尽管他有点暴躁。

turns.en: He's cantankerous, yeah.
turns.zh: 他脾气暴躁，是的。

turns.en: Yeah, he's cantankerous. He annoys people for sure. But you know, you want to hear the truth, you gotta be prepared to be hurt a little bit. He had a great tweet recently that I retweeted, which I'm sure made a lot of people mad, where he said: 'The opposite of education is not ignorance. The opposite of education is an education in social science.' I thought that was hilarious. But it hurts because there's some truth to it. If there was no truth to it, it wouldn't hurt at all. If I said 'Tim Ferriss is fat,' that would just bounce right off of you. But if I said 'Tim Ferriss and all of the fake gurus,' that might hit us right because we...
turns.zh: 是的，他脾气暴躁。他肯定惹恼了很多人。但你知道，你想听真话，就得准备好受点伤。他最近发了一条很棒的推文，我转发了，肯定让很多人恼火，他说：'教育的对立面不是无知。教育的对立面是接受社会科学教育。' 我觉得这很搞笑。但这话伤人，因为其中有些道理。如果完全没有道理，就不会伤人。如果我说'蒂姆·费里斯很胖'，那只会从你身上弹开。但如果我说'蒂姆·费里斯和所有假大师'，那可能会击中我们，因为我们……

turns.en: I suspect there's some deep level, there's a part of that that's true. So I think the same way, like with social science, it does kind of lead you down the road to ignorance because it's about social, and anything social is about groupthink and group behavior. Individuals can search for truth, but groups search for consensus, because if a group doesn't have consensus, it can't get along. If it can't get along, it falls apart and there's conflict. So the last place you're going to find truth is in large groups. And the moment you make science about large groups and about voting and about consensus, you're not really practicing science anymore.
turns.zh: 我怀疑在某个深层面上，有一部分是真实的。所以我认为，就像社会科学一样，它确实会把你引向无知的道路，因为它关乎社会，而任何社会性的东西都关乎群体思维和群体行为。个体可以追求真理，但群体追求的是共识，因为如果一个群体没有共识，就无法和睦相处。如果无法和睦相处，它就会分崩离析，产生冲突。所以，你最后能找到真理的地方就是大群体。一旦你把科学变成关于大群体、关于投票和共识的东西，你就不是在真正实践科学了。

turns.en: Yeah, and the science with a capital S is dangerous, very dangerous. It's propaganda. It then becomes like macroeconomics again. Another quote: 'It's easier to macro than it is to micro.' So big science is the equivalent of macroeconomics, whereas the equivalent of microeconomics is little science, which is me in a lab or me with my computer trying out various things. Just like with macroeconomics, you can pick and choose whatever data you want to prove whatever point you want. With big science, you can pick or choose whatever papers you want. Look at the fights right now on the internet about what COVID means. You can go down that hall of mirrors and find scientific, quote-unquote, substantiation for many points of view, depending on which papers you choose to cherry-pick. So then we reduce back to consensus. But again, science isn't done by consensus. It should be done by experimentation, verifiability, falsifiability, rigorous testing. In an ideal science world, you don't go and survey 10,000 scientists; you actually pick the one smartest scientist. But that's very hard to do now. No one can agree on who the smartest scientist is. So unfortunately, I think the nature of what science is is being corrupted, and that started the day we let the so-called social sciences masquerade as sciences. The day we said psychology is a science, and politics can be a science, and so on, and you can apply the scientific methods to those things, then I think that's the day we start to corrupt science, or at least the word science. So real science these days is relegated to a very small corner: physics, maybe molecular biology, chemistry, mathematics, theory of computation. But it's a fairly limited set.
turns.zh: 是的，大写 S 的科学是危险的，非常危险。那是宣传。它又变得像宏观经济学一样。另一句名言是：‘宏观比微观容易。’所以大科学相当于宏观经济学，而小科学相当于微观经济学，就是我在实验室里或电脑前尝试各种东西。就像宏观经济学一样，你可以挑选任何数据来证明任何观点。对于大科学，你可以挑选任何论文。看看现在互联网上关于 COVID 含义的争论。你可以走进那个镜子大厅，找到所谓的科学证据来支持许多观点，取决于你选择哪些论文来摘樱桃。于是我们又回到了共识。但科学不是靠共识来做的。它应该通过实验、可验证性、可证伪性、严格测试来完成。在理想的科学世界里，你不会去调查一万名科学家，而是挑选最聪明的那一位。但现在这很难做到。没人能就谁是最聪明的科学家达成一致。所以不幸的是，我认为科学的本质正在被腐蚀，这始于我们让所谓的社会科学伪装成科学的那一天。当我们说心理学是一门科学，政治学可以是一门科学，等等，并且你可以把科学方法应用到这些东西上时，我认为那就是我们开始腐蚀科学，或者至少是‘科学’这个词的那一天。所以真正的科学如今被局限在一个很小的角落里：物理学，也许还有分子生物学、化学、数学、计算理论。但这是一个相当有限的集合。

turns.en: Yeah, yeah. A very quantitatively literate friend of mine said at one point: most of the disciplines that have 'science' at the end of them are not actually science. All right, some exceptions. Computer science? Computer science is science if it's hard. Neuroscience might be an exception. But very often, if there's 'science' appended to it, it is not in fact something that is falsifiable according to someone like Karl Popper. It cannot be proven or disproven using experiment. At least that's right. If there isn't some experiment that could theoretically disprove it, then it's not science.
turns.zh: 是的，是的。我有一位非常精通定量分析的朋友曾说过：大多数以‘科学’结尾的学科实际上并不是科学。好吧，有些例外。计算机科学？如果它是硬科学，那就是科学。神经科学可能是个例外。但很多时候，如果后面加了‘科学’，它实际上并不是像卡尔·波普尔所说的那样可证伪的。它无法通过实验被证明或证伪。至少这是对的。如果不存在某种理论上可以证伪它的实验，那它就不是科学。

turns.en: It's like Honest Abe's law firm. If he needs to put 'honest' in the title, then you have to wonder. So the same way, if they put 'science' in the word, other than as you said computer science and neuroscience, you sort of have to wonder.
turns.zh: 这就像‘诚实的亚伯’律师事务所。如果他需要在名称里加上‘诚实’，那你就得怀疑了。同样，如果他们在词里加上‘科学’，除了你说的计算机科学和神经科学，你也得怀疑。

ts.sec: Knowing the Name vs. Knowing the Thing

turns.en: Let's look at a couple of quotes from Feynman. There are a few that I think about a lot. Number one, and I think this is really applicable with respect to science in media, particularly a term that bothers me a lot: 'studies prove' or 'studies show,' just like capital-S 'science shows,' which isn't really a thing. The facts can get contorted quite a lot. The quote from Feynman is: 'I learned very early the difference between knowing the name of something and knowing something.'
turns.zh: 我们来看看费曼的几句名言。有几句话我经常思考。第一句，我认为这非常适用于媒体中的科学，尤其是一个让我很困扰的术语：‘研究证明’或‘研究表明’，就像大写 S 的‘科学表明’，这其实不是一回事。事实可以被严重扭曲。费曼的这句话是：‘我很早就学会了知道某物的名字和知道某物之间的区别。’

turns.en: Yeah, this is a very deep point. I think that a lot of times we just define something with another definition, or we just throw out a piece of jargon as if that means we know something. It's a difference between memorization and understanding. Understanding is the thing that you want. You want to be able to describe it in ten different ways in simple sentences from the ground up, and rederive whatever you need. If you just memorize, you're lost. So I think this is one of the things that I get stuck on a lot: just keep going back and reading the basics over and over, trying to understand them. Don't necessarily have that tall edifice of knowledge that is very seductive in academia. But as anyone who's operated much in the real world knows, in the real world you get paid for making good judgments and decisions based on the basics. For example, if you know arithmetic, statistics, probability at a high level, and you understand some basic math, you have all the math that you need to succeed in life. You don't need calculus or even trigonometry or number theory or set theory or any of these kinds of things, unless you're going into a deeply technical field or mathematics. Yet we spend so much of our time in school covering pre-calc and calculus when the kids who are taking those courses aren't even that good at basic math. So the basics are really important. That's the steel frame, the foundation for understanding that you need to get through life. Too much of it is reduced to memorization and understanding things as names and as definitions. I think the Feynman example: his dad would take him for walks and they would see birds. His dad would say, 'This bird is such and such a warbler.' But that doesn't tell you anything about the bird. That tells you about humans. Humans gave that bird that name. Really, the bird likes to stand on one leg at this point, it likes to pick lice from its feathers, it likes to eat these kinds of things, it flies this way for that reason, these are its predators, these are its prey. Those are the kinds of things that really give you understanding. Who cares what the bird is called? The name of the bird is irrelevant. You see a lot of professions in professional life where this happens a lot: jargon. People try to protect their knowledge by basically saying, 'Well, I understand this term and that term and what this term means.' But the reality is most people don't actually know what these things mean underneath. I find this very common in accounting. A lot of business people don't know accounting, and even a lot of accountants will have fancy words for things, but when you dig underneath, they don't really understand the principle of what's going on below it. If you just understand the principles, even if you don't understand the words, you will have an advantage in, for example, negotiating deals, because you'll understand underneath how the pieces are actually moving on the board, as opposed to what they're called.
turns.zh: 是的，这是一个非常深刻的观点。我认为很多时候我们只是用另一个定义来定义某物，或者只是抛出一个术语，好像那就意味着我们知道某物。这是记忆和理解之间的区别。理解才是你想要的。你希望能够用简单的句子从基础开始用十种不同的方式描述它，并重新推导出你需要的任何东西。如果你只是记忆，你就迷失了。所以我认为这是我经常纠结的事情之一：不断回头，一遍又一遍地阅读基础，试图理解它们。不一定非要拥有那种在学术界非常诱人的知识大厦。但任何在现实世界中运作过的人都知道，在现实世界中，你因为基于基础做出良好的判断和决策而获得报酬。例如，如果你高水平地掌握算术、统计学、概率论，并且理解一些基础数学，你就拥有了在生活中成功所需的所有数学。你不需要微积分，甚至不需要三角学、数论或集合论之类的东西，除非你进入一个深度技术领域或数学。然而，我们在学校里花了那么多时间学习微积分预备和微积分，而选修这些课程的学生甚至不擅长基础数学。所以基础非常重要。那是钢铁框架，是你一生所需的理解基础。太多东西被简化为记忆，以及把事物理解为名称和定义。我认为费曼的例子：他父亲会带他散步，他们会看到鸟。他父亲会说，‘这只鸟是某种莺。’但这并没有告诉你任何关于鸟的事情。它告诉你的是关于人类的事情。人类给那只鸟起了那个名字。实际上，那只鸟喜欢在这个时候单腿站立，喜欢从羽毛里捉虱子，喜欢吃这些东西，它这样飞是因为那个原因，这些是它的天敌，这些是它的猎物。这些才是真正让你理解的东西。谁在乎那只鸟叫什么？鸟的名字无关紧要。你在职业生涯中看到很多行业都经常发生这种情况：行话。人们试图通过说‘嗯，我理解这个术语和那个术语以及这个术语的意思’来保护他们的知识。但现实是，大多数人实际上并不了解这些事物底层的含义。我发现这在会计中非常常见。很多商业人士不懂会计，甚至很多会计师对事物有花哨的词汇，但当你深入挖掘时，他们并不真正理解底层的原理。如果你只是理解原理，即使你不懂那些词汇，你也会在例如谈判交易中拥有优势，因为你会理解底层棋子实际上是如何在棋盘上移动的，而不是它们叫什么。

turns.en: I just want to point out that developing that...
turns.zh: 我只想指出，培养那种……

turns.en: Foundational knowledge is akin to learning the basics of shooting foul shots in basketball. It is not becoming Michael Jordan. With a very limited set of colors with which to paint, if you spend a few weeks—maybe not even a few weeks, a few days—with a decent YouTube tutorial or book gathering the basics of accounting, you will be able to make significantly better decisions. I just want to underscore that this is not a master's degree or PhD or any type of commitment like that that can separate you from the pack in terms of advantage. Running a business will teach you better accounting than a textbook. Now, you may need to learn certain conventions because you need those to communicate with other humans so that there's efficient communication. When you say one thing, they understand that thing. That's where jargon can be useful because it can be a compressed way of communicating knowledge rather than having to try and explain it from first principles every time. But when it's not useful is when it becomes a substitute for understanding. Understanding is a thing. That's what separates humans from other animals. That's what separates us from the robots: that we actually understand what's going on underneath. For example, in the artificial intelligence world, there's a new algorithm, GPT-3, which is the rage, which is text completion. So people are saying, "Hey, I can make up really plausible-sounding writing, and so the AI is here and is going to take all the jobs." Well, no, because the AI doesn't actually know what's going on underneath. If I were to ask a question, "Why did you write that?" or "What does that mean?" it would very quickly fall apart. So you always want to strive for understanding, not for memorization. You should be able to rearticulate it five different ways in every language that you know; otherwise, you don't really understand it. And the good news is you don't have to understand that many things.
turns.zh: 基础知识就像学习篮球罚球的基本动作，而不是成为迈克尔·乔丹。用非常有限的色彩作画，如果你花几周——甚至不用几周，几天——通过一个不错的 YouTube 教程或一本书掌握会计基础，你就能做出明显更好的决策。我想强调，这不需要硕士学位或博士学位，也不需要任何那种能让你脱颖而出的承诺。经营企业比教科书更能教会你会计。不过，你可能需要学习某些惯例，因为你需要它们来与他人高效沟通。当你说一件事时，他们能理解。这就是行话有用的地方，因为它可以压缩知识，而不必每次都从头解释。但行话没用的时候，是当它成为理解的替代品时。理解才是关键。那是人类区别于其他动物的地方，也是我们区别于机器人的地方：我们真正理解表面之下的东西。例如，在人工智能领域，有一个新算法 GPT-3，它很热门，用于文本补全。所以人们说：“嘿，我能写出非常逼真的文章，AI 来了，它会抢走所有工作。”不，因为 AI 并不真正理解表面之下的东西。如果我问：“你为什么写这个？”或“这是什么意思？”它很快就会露馅。所以你总是要追求理解，而不是死记硬背。你应该能用你知道的每种语言以五种不同方式重新表述它；否则，你并没有真正理解它。好消息是，你不需要理解太多东西。

turns.en: While we're on the topic, Feynman has a quote: "Nature uses only the longest threads to weave her tapestry." What he means is that there are only a few basic principles you have to understand to grasp the sum total of our knowledge in physics. There's not a lot. And as physics becomes more advanced, we're actually unifying theories, collapsing them into one thing, like we did with electricity and magnetism, or statistical mechanics and heat and thermodynamics. Those turn out to be one thing. We're trying to do that currently with gravity and quantum mechanics, reconciling them in quantum loop gravity or string theory. So you just have to understand a few basic things, and then you'll see them recurring over and over. For example, I study and read physics and computer science just for entertainment. There's no practical application for me. But there's this really interesting theory I was reading about called Solomonoff induction. It's basically formalized as Occam's razor: the shortest answer is the correct one. But it's more advanced than that. It says if you want to figure out why something happened, you take all the different reasons it could have happened, all the different theories, and then you weigh each of them based on how compressed they are, how short they are, how likely they are. That gives you the probability, which gives you the explanation for why that theory happened. Feynman has a very similar and elegant thing in a completely different field. He says if you want to understand why this particle took that path, why this photon took that path, you look at all the possible paths that photon could have taken and you weigh them, summing them probabilistically based on how likely that path was. It's actually the same observation, except one is about photons traveling through space-time and the other is about causality and why an event may have happened and the theory behind that. These are two completely different fields and two completely different characters thinking about two completely different things. These are very fundamental theories in their fields, but deep down they're actually the same. To me, that's fascinating. It just shows how much of what we look at as complexity actually emerges from a set of very simple rules. So understanding is a thing to strive for, not the words. Even though you use fancy words like Solomonoff induction and Feynman's path integral theory—whatever it's called, I don't even know the actual name—the name is irrelevant. It's the concept that's fascinating.
turns.zh: 说到这个话题，费曼有句名言：“大自然只用最长的线来编织她的挂毯。”他的意思是，要理解物理学的全部知识，你只需要掌握几个基本原理。并不多。随着物理学越来越先进，我们实际上在统一理论，把它们合并成一个东西，就像我们对电和磁，或者统计力学与热力学所做的那样。那些结果证明是一回事。目前我们正试图对引力和量子力学做同样的事，在圈量子引力或弦理论中调和它们。所以你只需要理解几个基本的东西，然后你会看到它们反复出现。例如，我学习物理和计算机科学只是为了娱乐，对我没有实际应用。但我读到过一个非常有趣的理论，叫做所罗门诺夫归纳法。它基本上被形式化为奥卡姆剃刀：最短的答案是正确的。但它比那更高级。它说，如果你想弄清楚某事发生的原因，你列出所有可能的原因，所有不同的理论，然后根据它们的压缩程度、简短程度和可能性来权衡每个理论。这给出了概率，也就给出了为什么那个理论成立的解释。费曼在一个完全不同的领域有一个非常相似且优雅的东西。他说，如果你想理解为什么这个粒子走了那条路径，为什么这个光子走了那条路径，你要看那个光子可能走的所有路径，然后根据每条路径的可能性进行概率求和。这实际上是同一个观察，只不过一个关于光子穿越时空，另一个关于因果关系以及事件发生的原因和背后的理论。这是两个完全不同的领域，两个完全不同的人物思考着完全不同的事情。这些是各自领域非常基础的理论，但深层次上它们其实是相同的。对我来说，这很迷人。它展示了我们视为复杂的东西有多少实际上是从一组非常简单的规则中涌现出来的。所以理解是值得追求的东西，而不是词汇。即使你使用像所罗门诺夫归纳法和费曼路径积分理论这样的花哨词汇——不管它叫什么，我甚至不知道确切的名字——名字无关紧要。概念本身才迷人。

ts.sec: How to Get Rich (Without Getting Lucky)

turns.en: You spoke to learning accounting through building a business. Let's segue to business. Your pin tweet is a tweet storm, a series of tweets, the headline of which is "How to Get Rich (Without Getting Lucky)." It has, as we record this, 44,800 retweets, 110,000-plus favorites. We're not going to go through this whole thing; it's quite long. But I'm curious to know what parts of it you think people are paying too much attention to or overemphasize, and what parts do you wish people would pay more attention to?
turns.zh: 你谈到了通过经营企业来学习会计。我们转到商业话题。你的置顶推文是一个推文风暴，一系列推文，标题是“如何致富（不靠运气）”。在我们录制时，它有 44,800 次转发，超过 11 万次点赞。我们不打算全部过一遍；它相当长。但我很好奇，你认为人们过于关注或过度强调其中的哪些部分？你希望人们更关注哪些部分？

turns.en: That tweet storm is a series of principles that I kind of wrote for myself in my head when I was 13, trying to figure out how to make money. I came up with a framework of how to be rich but not by accident, to do it in a way that I could repeat over and over, leaving very little up to chance. Because I think there's this belief—it's not completely mythology—that to make money you either have to be born rich, or privileged, or in the right circles, or get lucky. I think there are still ways to accomplish the original American dream: make money, but do it in a deliberate, systematic way. When I say money, I mean wealth. I don't mean like a law firm where you make a couple hundred bucks an hour but you're still tied to the clock. I mean when you wake up when you want, go to sleep when you want, live where you want, and have freedom. To me, the purpose of money is freedom. For that, you need to create wealth. Can you do it ethically? Can you do it sustainably? Can you do it reliably? Can you do it with people that you like? Can you do it doing things that you enjoy? I think it's absolutely possible. I'd like to think that I'm at least one of many living proof points for that. Warren Buffett and Charlie Munger are examples of others—not that I'm in their status.
turns.zh: 那个推文风暴是一系列原则，我 13 岁时在脑子里为自己写的，试图弄清楚如何赚钱。我提出了一个如何致富但不是偶然致富的框架，以一种可以重复的方式，几乎不留给运气。因为我认为有一种信念——不完全是神话——要赚钱，你要么生来富有，要么有特权，要么在合适的圈子里，要么走运。我认为仍然有办法实现最初的美国梦：赚钱，但以深思熟虑、系统的方式。当我说钱时，我指的是财富。我不是指像律师事务所那样每小时赚几百美元，但仍然被时间束缚。我指的是你想醒就醒，想睡就睡，想住哪就住哪，拥有自由。对我来说，钱的目的就是自由。为此，你需要创造财富。你能合乎道德地做吗？能可持续地做吗？能可靠地做吗？能和你喜欢的人一起做吗？能做你喜欢的事情吗？我认为这完全可能。我愿意相信我是众多活生生的例证之一。沃伦·巴菲特和查理·芒格是其他人的例子——不是说我有他们的地位。

turns.en: Of their league, but you know, other people have done it. So that was the whole point of that framework and the tweetstorm. I just wrote it down one day. And it's funny because obviously I have some experience at Twitter, so I know how to craft things. But I think that was written back in the day when the tweet limit was 140 characters too, so it's harder. But I just woke up one night when I've been thinking about it, and I wrote up the whole tweetstorm almost exactly like you see it. And there are lots and lots of missing pieces. Obviously, that is just like a very high-level, very zen-like, or haiku-like, or even Hallmark card-like framework. It's missing a ton of details, which I then tried to extrapolate on in my podcast series on the same topic.
turns.zh: 他们不在一个水平上，但你知道，其他人已经做到了。所以这就是那个框架和推文风暴的全部意义。我只是某天把它写了下来。有趣的是，显然我在推特上有些经验，所以我知道如何精心设计内容。但我认为那是在推文字符限制还是 140 个字符的时代写的，所以更难。但我只是某天晚上醒来，一直在想这件事，然后几乎一字不差地写下了整个推文风暴。而且有很多很多缺失的部分。显然，那只是一个非常高层、非常禅意、或俳句式、甚至贺卡式的框架。它缺少了大量细节，我后来在关于同一主题的播客系列中试图阐述这些细节。

turns.en: What do people overemphasize and what are people missing? And actually, let me pause to just read a few of them. This is from May 31, 2018. So a few examples, and you don't have to comment on these specific examples, but just for people who haven't seen this, I want to give them an idea. So the second of these various tweets in this sequence is: 'Understand that ethical wealth creation is possible. If you secretly despise wealth, it will elude you.' End of that particular tweet. And then there's another one: 'You're not going to get rich renting out your time. You must own equity — a piece of a business — to gain your financial freedom.' That one right there is the most important one.
turns.zh: 人们过度强调什么，又忽略了什么？实际上，让我暂停一下，读几个例子。这是 2018 年 5 月 31 日的。所以有几个例子，你不必评论这些具体的例子，但只是为了给没看过的人一个概念。这一系列推文中的第二条是：‘理解道德财富创造是可能的。如果你暗中鄙视财富，它会避开你。’那条推文到此结束。然后还有一条：‘你不会通过出租时间致富。你必须拥有股权——企业的一部分——才能获得财务自由。’那一条是最重要的。

turns.en: Which is, you basically, in modern life, what happens is the person who is the best at doing something in the world will get to do it for the entire world through a combination of leverage and distribution, accountability, and specific knowledge, all of which I talk about in the tweetstorm. If you're the best in the world at doing something — like if you're the best teacher in the world at math, you should be teaching the entire world math. If you're the best podcast interviewer, you should be doing all the top interviews. And the returns will accrue to you, especially in this highly digital world where we live in, where the cost of distributing something is very close to zero. And so what you kind of want to do is you want to productize yourself into a business, and then you want to own that business. That is the way to make wealth.
turns.zh: 基本上，在现代生活中，发生的事情是：世界上在某方面最擅长的人，将通过杠杆和分销、问责制和特定知识的结合，为整个世界做这件事——我在推文风暴中都谈到了。如果你是世界上最擅长做某事的人——比如你是世界上最好的数学老师，你应该教全世界的数学。如果你是最好的播客采访者，你应该做所有顶级采访。回报将归于你，尤其是在我们这个高度数字化的世界里，分销成本几乎为零。所以你想做的是，把自己产品化成一个企业，然后拥有那个企业。这就是创造财富的方式。

turns.en: A clear example is the Tim Ferriss podcast, the Tim Ferriss brand, right? It's an eponymous brand. Your name is on it. You're leveraged through this podcast and through the books that you write and through the army of followers that you have on the various media platforms. And you're taking on big accountability, and there's specific knowledge: only you know how to be Tim Ferriss, the learning machine, who can then connect to other learners and extract value out of them and share that with the audience. So that's an example of you having productized yourself, but ultimately you own the Tim Ferriss business.
turns.zh: 一个明显的例子是蒂姆·费里斯的播客，蒂姆·费里斯品牌，对吧？这是一个同名品牌。你的名字在上面。你通过这个播客、你写的书以及你在各个媒体平台上拥有的粉丝大军获得了杠杆。你承担了巨大的责任，并且有特定知识：只有你知道如何成为蒂姆·费里斯，这个学习机器，然后连接其他学习者，从中提取价值并与观众分享。所以这是一个你将自己产品化的例子，但最终你拥有蒂姆·费里斯这个企业。

turns.en: Now you can go lower levels down from that. You don't have to own the entire business. You could be an investor in public equities or private stocks. You could be a partner in a private business. You could be an employee at a startup where you have stock options. But if you don't own a piece of a business, it's going to be extremely hard to get wealthy. It'll be nearly impossible, almost not worth that route. So I think that is the most important foundational tweet.
turns.zh: 现在你可以从那里往下走。你不必拥有整个企业。你可以是公开股票或私人股票的投资人。你可以是私营企业的合伙人。你可以是初创公司的员工，拥有股票期权。但如果你不拥有一部分企业，致富将极其困难。几乎不可能，几乎不值得走那条路。所以我认为那是最重要的基础推文。

turns.en: I think where people kind of all miss the plot the most, though, is we have pain avoidance in life. We don't want to face things where it's clear we've made bad decisions. Because one good definition of suffering is that's the moment when you see things as they clearly are, and you kind of don't like what you see. For example, if you and your spouse have been fighting for a long time, you know you've kind of been sweeping it under the rug, and you've sort of been suffering along, but you're sweeping it under the rug. And then you get divorced. At that moment, you can no longer unsee all the damage that was in the relationship and what all the consequences are. They're real. The same way, if you haven't been taking care of your health, but then suddenly you find out that you've just fallen off a cliff and something irreversibly bad has happened, the moment of suffering arrives and you're in pain because you can no longer unsee this thing, you can no longer avoid it.
turns.zh: 我认为人们最误解的地方在于，我们在生活中会避免痛苦。我们不想面对那些明显做出错误决定的事情。因为痛苦的一个很好的定义是：当你清楚地看到事情本来的样子，而你不喜欢你所看到的那个时刻。例如，如果你和你的配偶争吵了很久，你知道你一直在掩盖问题，你一直在忍受，但你把它掩盖了。然后你们离婚了。那一刻，你再也无法忽视关系中所有的伤害以及所有的后果。它们是真实的。同样，如果你没有照顾好自己的健康，但突然你发现自己已经跌下悬崖，发生了不可逆转的坏事，痛苦的时刻到来了，你感到痛苦，因为你再也无法忽视这件事，再也无法逃避它。

turns.en: So I think the same way. The unfortunate part is that a lot of these principles are written for young people, because you can set yourself on a certain trajectory in life earlier. And so, for example, if you went and got a PhD in a social science and now I'm basically saying, 'Hey, learn to code,' you're going to avoid that. You're not going to want to see that because you have all this sunk cost in the degree. So I think a lot of people don't want to pick up the new skills that are necessary, or they don't want to, for example, physically move, or they don't want to disappoint the people in the relationships that they already have to make room for new relationships. So everybody wants to start where they are. Nobody wants to go back down the mountain to find the path going to the top. Everybody wants to stay on the path they're on, maybe make a few tweaks, and get to the top. Or like Charlie Munger jokes: 'People always ask me, how do I get to be rich like you, except quicker? I don't want to be the old rich guy, I want to be the young rich guy.'
turns.zh: 所以我认为同样如此。不幸的是，很多这些原则是为年轻人写的，因为你可以更早地让自己走上某条人生轨迹。所以，例如，如果你已经获得了社会科学的博士学位，而我现在基本上说，‘嘿，学编程吧’，你会回避。你不想看到这一点，因为你在学位上投入了这么多沉没成本。所以我认为很多人不想学习必要的新技能，或者他们不想，例如，搬家，或者他们不想让现有关系中的人失望，以便为新关系腾出空间。所以每个人都想从他们所在的地方开始。没有人想下山去寻找通往山顶的路。每个人都想留在他们现在的路上，也许做一些调整，然后到达山顶。或者像查理·芒格开玩笑说的：‘人们总是问我，我怎么才能像你一样富有，只是更快？我不想成为老富翁，我想成为年轻的富翁。’

ts.sec: The Hard Part: Unlearning and Starting Over

turns.en: So I think these are the hard parts. The hard parts are not the learning, it's the unlearning. It's not the climbing up the mountain, it's the going back down to the bottom of the mountain and starting over. It's the beginner's mind that every great artist or every great business person has, which is you have to be willing to start from scratch. You have to be willing to hit reset and go back to zero, because you have to realize that what you already know and what you're already doing is actually an impediment to your full potential. And most people just don't want to acknowledge that. And I'm guilty of that too. That's just human nature. I'm not faulting anybody for it, but it's just human nature.
turns.zh: 所以我认为这些是困难的部分。困难的部分不是学习，而是忘却。不是爬山，而是下山回到山脚重新开始。这是每个伟大艺术家或伟大商人都拥有的初学者心态，即你必须愿意从零开始。你必须愿意重置并回到零，因为你必须意识到你已经知道的和已经在做的事情实际上是你充分发挥潜力的障碍。而大多数人就是不想承认这一点。我也犯过这个错。这只是人性。我不是在责怪任何人，但这只是人性。

Did you know if you missed 10 of the best performing days after the 2008 crisis, you would have missed out on 50% of your returns? Don't miss out on the best days in the market. Stay invested in a long-term automated investment portfolio. Wealthfront pioneered the automated investing movement, sometimes referred to as robo-advising, and they currently oversee $20 billion of assets for their clients. Wealthfront can help you diversify your portfolio, minimize fees, and lower your taxes. It takes about three minutes to sign up, and then Wealthfront will build you a globally diversified portfolio of ETFs based on your risk appetite and manage it for you at an incredibly low cost. Wealthfront's software constantly monitors your portfolio day in and day out.
turns.zh: 快速感谢我们的一个赞助商，我们马上回来继续节目。本期节目由 Wealthfront 赞助。你知道吗，如果你错过了 2008 年危机后表现最好的 10 天，你就会错过 50%的回报？不要错过市场最好的日子。保持投资于长期自动化投资组合。Wealthfront 开创了自动化投资运动，有时被称为机器人顾问，他们目前为客户管理着 200 亿美元的资产。Wealthfront 可以帮助你分散投资组合、最小化费用并降低税收。注册大约需要三分钟，然后 Wealthfront 会根据你的风险偏好为你建立一个全球多元化的 ETF 投资组合，并以极低的成本为你管理。Wealthfront 的软件日复一日地持续监控你的投资组合。

turns.en: You don't have to; they look for opportunities to rebalance and tax loss harvest to lower the amount of taxes you pay on your investment gains. Their newest service is called Autopilot, and it can monitor any checking account for excess cash to move into savings or an investment account. They've really thought of a ton; they've checked a lot of boxes. Smart investing should not feel like a roller coaster ride. Let the professionals do the work for you. Go to wealthfront.com/tim and open a Wealthfront investment account today, and you'll get your first $5,000 managed for free for life. That's wealthfront.com/tim. Wealthfront will automate your investments for the long term, and you can get started today at wealthfront.com/tim.
turns.zh: 你不必操心；他们会寻找再平衡和税收损失收割的机会，以降低你投资收益的纳税额。他们的最新服务叫 Autopilot，可以监控任何支票账户，将多余现金转入储蓄或投资账户。他们考虑得非常周全，满足了很多需求。明智的投资不应像坐过山车。让专业人士为你代劳。访问 wealthfront.com/tim，今天开设 Wealthfront 投资账户，你的前 5000 美元将终身免费管理。网址是 wealthfront.com/tim。Wealthfront 会为你的长期投资自动化管理，今天就可以在 wealthfront.com/tim 开始。

ts.sec: Applying the Principle of Productizing Yourself

turns.en: And if we look at your specific case as it relates to equity, you mentioned owning part of a business. You also mentioned in this conversation productizing yourself. So would you view your success in following that principle predominantly in, for instance, the equity that you own in a company like AngelList, or is it the identity and the brand, meaning associations that you've built as Naval the human, or is it 'slash investor' therefore being sought after as an investor? Or is it something else? How would you think of that principle as applying most in your own life, because you've created wealth not just through, say, your equity in AngelList, but also through many successful investments, and then I'm sure there are other ways that we could identify wealth as coming from your path.
turns.zh: 那么，看看你个人的情况，关于股权，你提到拥有企业的一部分。你还在这次对话中提到了‘把自己产品化’。那么，你认为自己遵循这一原则的成功主要体现在哪里？比如，你在 AngelList 这样的公司拥有的股权，还是你作为 Naval 这个人建立的身份和品牌，即各种关联，还是作为‘斜杠投资者’因而被追捧？还是其他方面？你如何看待这一原则在你生活中的应用？因为你不仅通过 AngelList 的股权创造了财富，还通过许多成功的投资，我相信还有其他方式可以识别出财富来自你的道路。

turns.en: Yeah, what I like about my path is that I've made money doing a lot of different little things. So I've made money consistently in sort of small to medium-sized chunks. I haven't had like one gigantic payday that set me up forever, although it depends on your standards, right? I'm talking about by Silicon Valley standards. Obviously, for most parts of the world, I've had many of those gigantic paydays, but they've been consistent and they've been varied. Varied in the sense that there are completely different kinds of investments and endeavors, but consistent in that I get one every couple of years.
turns.zh: 是的，我喜欢自己道路的一点是，我通过许多不同的小事赚到了钱。所以我持续地赚到中小额度的钱。我没有那种一次性的巨额收入让我一劳永逸，尽管这取决于你的标准，对吧？我是按硅谷的标准说的。显然，对世界大部分地区来说，我已经有过很多次那样的巨额收入了，但它们一直持续且多样化。多样化是指完全不同的投资和努力，但持续是指每几年就有一笔。

turns.en: Which would seem to suggest that there are principles guiding your approaches, or...
turns.zh: 这似乎表明有原则在指导你的方法，或者……

turns.en: Absolutely right. Therefore, yeah, you're not relying on winning the Powerball lottery or that equivalent.
turns.zh: 完全正确。因此，是的，你不是靠中强力球彩票或类似的东西。

turns.en: No, not at all.
turns.zh: 不，完全不是。

turns.en: No, there's no lottery here. To win the lottery is for losers. Lotteries are just attacks on people who can't do math. Yet rich get rich quick schemes are just other people getting rich off of you. There are no shortcuts. So what I'm doing is I'm taking my specific knowledge, which is my ability to understand deeply technical concepts and communicate them to the rest of the world, to be an interface between great programmers and developers and designers and the capital markets and consumers, and using that to put myself in a position where then I can identify great trends as they're emerging, invest in those companies or help start those companies, own equity in those things, help bring them to market, help do the strategy on how to navigate the worlds of fundraising and exiting and recruiting and company building and cultures and technology development and all that, and have a brand around it. And I use that to kind of make money. And that is just one of several ways. You know, I've also done it by investing early on in public markets and cryptocurrencies and starting funds and all kinds of things. But I don't, it's funny, I don't really follow my own principles anymore. Like, for example, the whole podcast thing and the whole Twitter thing, even though I've got reasonably good brands and followers and those, I'm not monetizing them at all. I'm not charging anybody for anything, because I'm not trying to make money anymore. To me, like making money is like you become the kind of person that makes money, and you put yourself in long-term situations where you're always going to make money. So I have the brand and I have deep relationships with a dozen people who I know and I trust that I can do business with for the rest of my life, and they're very high integrity people, they're very capable people, and just makes it easy to do things. So I've set up that infrastructure. Now the money just kind of makes itself as I go about my life. I don't want to have to work hard. I don't have to roll out of bed at a certain time. I don't have to answer to somebody. So I optimize for independence and freedom. I could have made a lot more money by raising a huge fund or joining a big VC firm early on or being exec at one of the massive companies in Silicon Valley early on, but I always optimize for independence. I'm lazy, you know. I wake up at 7, 8, 9, 10 a.m., I go to sleep at 2:30 a.m., you know. I don't work a lot of days. Some days I work morning to night, but it's just based on whatever I'm curious about. I never want to have to answer to a boss. No one's ever going to tell me what to do. I don't want to order people around. I don't want to have someone reporting to me and kind of asking me all the time what to do. I want peer relationships. I want to flow. I want to be able to do business while walking, you know, in a forest talking on a cell phone, or sitting in a meeting or in front of a computer, or I want to be on a beach. If I don't feel like it, the ideal would be to make money with your mind, not with your time. So if I can just make one good decision a year and that makes me all the money that I need for that year, then that's perfect. And that's the way it should be, because we're living in an age of infinite leverage, and your judgment just gets multiplied through this massive force multiplier through code, capital, community, labor, what have you. So if you're smart and you kind of know what you're doing, you don't need to work hard. Working hard is the last, least important thing. You have to pay your dues, you have to put in all the iterations, ten thousand iterations, not ten thousand hours, to figure out what to do and how to do it well. But once you know, you don't have to put in the time anymore. It's your judgment, and the judgment comes from clear thinking. The clear thinking comes from having time to reflect and to pursue your genuine intellectual curiosity. And you'll see that a lot of the great investors, for example, just sit around reading most of the time, or Warren Buffett famously plays a lot of bridge. Obviously, not everyone can be just an investor, although that is becoming more and more democratized. And I've picked something that suits my temperament and particular capabilities. But there's lots and lots of ways to make money if you apply your mind to it. So I don't work to make money. I mean, if I make so much money that I'm donating hospital wings and universities and all that stuff, I overshot, you know. I'm not trying to have some influence on the world through money. And ironically, I have more influence on the world through podcasts like this. And so, you know, making more money...
turns.zh: 不，这里没有彩票。中彩票是给失败者的。彩票只是对不会算数的人的攻击。而快速致富的计划只是别人从你身上致富。没有捷径。所以我所做的是，运用我的特定知识，即我深入理解技术概念并将其传达给世界的能力，成为优秀程序员、开发者、设计师与资本市场和消费者之间的接口，并利用这一点将自己置于能够识别新兴趋势的位置，投资或帮助创办这些公司，拥有股权，帮助它们推向市场，制定融资、退出、招聘、公司建设、文化和技术发展等战略，并围绕它建立品牌。我用这个来赚钱。这只是其中一种方式。你知道，我还通过早期投资公开市场、加密货币、创办基金等各种方式赚钱。但有趣的是，我不再真正遵循自己的原则了。比如，整个播客和 Twitter 的事情，尽管我有相当不错的品牌和粉丝，但我完全没有将其货币化。我不向任何人收费，因为我不再试图赚钱了。对我来说，赚钱就像你成为那种会赚钱的人，然后把自己置于长期能赚钱的境地。所以我有了品牌，与十几个我认识且信任的人建立了深厚关系，我可以和他们做一辈子生意，他们是非常正直、非常有能力的人，这让事情变得容易。我已经建立了这个基础设施。现在钱随着我的生活自然而然地来了。我不想努力工作。我不必在某个时间起床。我不必向任何人汇报。所以我优化的是独立和自由。我本可以通过筹集巨额基金、早期加入大型风投公司或成为硅谷大公司的高管来赚更多的钱，但我总是优化独立。我很懒，你知道。我早上 7、8、9、10 点起床，凌晨 2:30 睡觉。我很多天不工作。有些天我从早工作到晚，但那只是基于我好奇什么。我永远不想向老板汇报。没有人能告诉我该做什么。我不想指挥别人。我不想有人向我汇报，总是问我该做什么。我想要对等的关系。我想要流动。我想要能一边走路一边做生意，比如在森林里打电话，或者坐在会议室或电脑前，或者我想在海滩上。如果我不想，理想的情况是用头脑赚钱，而不是用时间。所以如果我一年只做一个好的决定，就能赚到那一年需要的所有钱，那就完美了。而且应该是这样的，因为我们生活在一个无限杠杆的时代，你的判断力通过代码、资本、社区、劳动力等巨大的力量倍增器被放大。所以如果你聪明，你知道自己在做什么，你不需要努力工作。努力工作是最后、最不重要的事情。你必须付出代价，你必须进行所有迭代，一万次迭代，而不是一万小时，来弄清楚该做什么以及如何做好。但一旦你知道，你就不再需要投入时间了。这是你的判断力，而判断力来自清晰的思考。清晰的思考来自有时间反思和追求你真正的好奇心。你会看到很多伟大的投资者，例如，大部分时间只是坐着阅读，或者沃伦·巴菲特以打桥牌闻名。显然，不是每个人都能只做投资者，尽管这变得越来越民主化。我选择了适合我性情和特定能力的事情。但如果你用心去思考，赚钱的方法有很多很多。所以我不是为了赚钱而工作。我的意思是，如果我赚了太多钱，以至于我在捐赠医院侧翼和大学之类的东西，那我就过头了。我不是想通过金钱对世界产生影响。讽刺的是，我通过像这样的播客对世界产生了更大的影响。所以，你知道，赚更多的钱……

turns.en: Let's talk about a few of your missives. Missives is probably not the right word.
turns.zh: 我们来聊聊你的一些言论吧。“言论”可能不太准确。

turns.en: I'm not telling you anything. I'm not trying to persuade anyone of anything. So if people don't like it, some people get triggered on Twitter and they start arguing. It's like just leave, just don't listen to it.
turns.zh: 我并没有在教导什么，也不是想说服任何人。如果有人不喜欢，有些人会在推特上被激怒然后开始争论。那就别听好了。

turns.en: The one I'm going to read would be a great one for people to get really upset about, or I would find it comical to look at the comments people are upset. But the line is this: 'Imagine how effective you would be if you weren't anxious all the time.' Why did you put this out? And have you seen highly anxious people become people who experience very little anxiety? Is that something you've seen? But first, why did you put this up? What was behind it? And then have you seen anxious people become largely unanxious or non-anxious people?
turns.zh: 我要读的这条可能会让很多人非常不爽，或者我觉得看那些生气的评论会很有趣。这句话是：‘想象一下，如果你不是一直焦虑，你会变得多高效。’你为什么发这个？你有没有见过高度焦虑的人变成几乎不焦虑的人？你见过这种情况吗？但首先，你为什么发这个？背后的原因是什么？然后你有没有见过焦虑的人变得基本上不焦虑了？

turns.en: Most of my tweets are not very deliberate or thought through. What's happening is I've been thinking about some concept for months, years, whatever, it's been percolating. Then all of a sudden it'll come to me in one spiffy sentence where I'm like, 'Oh, this is how I will remember this thing. This is how I can distill this down into a pointer for myself so that when I need to make decisions, I can retrieve that whole line of reasoning.' So where this comes from is basically like everybody after a certain age, I am taking responsibility for my own happiness and peace and quality of life. One of the things you learn after you make money is that money doesn't make you happier. It takes care of your money problems, but it's not necessarily going to put you in a place where you're in some kind of bliss all the time. In fact, there's nothing out there that will make you happy forever. So you sort of have to take responsibility for guiding yourself in such a way that your mental state ends up where you want it. So I've been working on my mental state, and I have—working is a big word, I don't want to say I've been working on it—but I would say my mental state has gotten to a place which is much better than it used to be. People will often say, 'Well, I don't want to do that because it will take away my ambition. I want to succeed right now.' So I've thought about that a fair bit. Is that true or not? Certainly for me, in the last few years since I've become calmer, my effectiveness has gone through the roof, and I've been more successful. But it's hard to disambiguate that from, 'Well, also maybe you're just later in your career, you're in a better position for it.' And that's a valid criticism. So one of the things I've been trying to figure out is, would I have been as successful? And it's hard to do the counterfactual, obviously. If I wasn't as anxious, because anxiety comes from fear, it's also a motivator. It makes you get off your butt. One of the ways to make the anxiety go away, at least until the next piece of anxiety comes along, is to go do something about it. So what is the role of anxiety? Well, firstly, I noted that pretty much everybody's anxious all the time. It's a rare human being who isn't anxious. And it makes sense: we're alpha predators. We took over this earth and domesticated or killed all the other animals, and we did that by being the most paranoid, the most fearful, the most angry predators this world has ever seen. So anxiety is built into the core of who we are. In fact, you could argue that all the mind does all day long is fear-based scenario planning for survival, and then maybe a little bit of greed for replication. So anxiety is at the core level of who we are. But certainly some people are more anxious than others, there's no question. And some people seem to be much calmer about it. So which is more useful for effectiveness? Assuming that your goal is your motivation is intrinsic—you're doing the thing because you love it or because you really want it—and you can separate that motivation from anxiety, then I think you can take on certain superpowers. And we kind of all intrinsically know this. If you look at samurai warriors, Miyamoto Musashi is in a duel with somebody else. The person who is calmer is going to win. In all those movies, it's the one who is incredibly still, then swipes with a sword incredibly quickly, and then still again. That's the winner. The one who has a zen state of mind. Similarly, in the Terminator movies, part of the reason you fear the Terminator is because he's a robot. He's unstoppable, he's implacable. You can't argue with him, you can't communicate with him, you can't make him slow down, and he has no remorse. He just keeps coming. Or in that old Clint Eastwood movie Unforgiven, the guy who wins the gunfight is the one who doesn't flinch. He's just keeping his cool while he's loading his gun and shooting. He's not all over the place running around. So I just think we waste so much energy through anxiety that if you can be calm and still go about your business, it's a superpower. I realized this myself recently where I was in a conflict situation in business. It was a high conflict situation, it was unfortunate. I think we've solved it. But as I went through this high conflict situation, I'd been through one like it years before where I was much more anxious. I remember that time period, I remember how much I was sweating it and how nervous I was, and how I went through bouts of fear and anger, and how kind of worked up I was the entire time, intense and didn't get much sleep. But this time I was incredibly calm, and I was almost enjoying it because it was like practicing my craft. Now, of course, it's easier to do now because I have more money, but at the same time it just didn't bother me. It was just very mechanical. And because of that, I could be very effective about it, and I could be effective about it while doing lots and lots of other things. My mind wasn't constantly spinning in a whirlpool, taking on 10 different problems and just fear-based airplane-ing all the time. Most of these things were never going to happen. So I do believe that being calm and still going about your business is the superpower. Now, yes, if anxiety is your only motivator, then you have a problem. But I would argue the pure motivations don't come out of anxiety.
turns.zh: 我的大多数推文都不是深思熟虑的。实际情况是，我一直在思考某个概念，几个月、几年，一直在酝酿。然后突然灵光一现，变成一句精炼的话，我就想：‘哦，这就是我记住这件事的方式。这就是我把它提炼成一个指向标的方法，这样当我需要做决定时，就能回忆起整个推理过程。’所以这条推文的来源是，基本上每个人到了一定年龄，都要为自己的幸福、平静和生活质量负责。赚钱后你学到的一件事是，钱不会让你更快乐。它解决了你的金钱问题，但不一定会让你一直处于某种幸福状态。事实上，没有什么能让你永远快乐。所以你必须负责引导自己，让你的精神状态达到你想要的境地。所以我一直在调整我的精神状态——‘调整’这个词可能有点重，我不想说我在‘努力’，但我的精神状态确实比以前好多了。人们常说：‘我不想那样做，因为那会削弱我的野心。我现在就想成功。’所以我对此思考了很多。这是真的吗？对我来说，过去几年我变得更平静后，效率确实突飞猛进，也更成功了。但很难排除另一种可能：‘也许只是因为你职业生涯后期，处境更好了。’这是个合理的质疑。所以我一直在想，如果我没有那么焦虑，我还会一样成功吗？显然，反事实很难验证。焦虑源于恐惧，它也是一种动力。它让你行动起来。缓解焦虑的方法之一——至少在下一波焦虑到来之前——就是去做点什么。那么焦虑的作用是什么？首先，我注意到几乎每个人都在焦虑。不焦虑的人很少见。这很合理：我们是顶级捕食者。我们统治了地球，驯化或杀死了所有其他动物，靠的就是成为这个星球上最偏执、最恐惧、最愤怒的捕食者。所以焦虑是我们核心的一部分。事实上，你可以说大脑整天做的就是基于恐惧的生存情景规划，再加上一点繁殖的贪婪。所以焦虑是我们的核心。但有些人确实比其他人更焦虑，这毫无疑问。有些人则显得平静得多。那么对于效率来说，哪种更有用？假设你的目标是内在驱动的——你做事是因为热爱或真心想要——并且你能把这种动力和焦虑分开，那么我认为你就能获得某些超能力。我们内心都知道这一点。看看武士，宫本武藏和别人决斗时，更冷静的人会赢。在所有那些电影里，赢家都是那个极其镇定、然后极快挥剑、然后又恢复镇定的人。那就是赢家，拥有禅宗心态的人。同样，在《终结者》电影里，你害怕终结者的部分原因是他是个机器人。他不可阻挡，冷酷无情。你无法和他争辩，无法沟通，无法让他慢下来，他没有悔意。他只是一直前进。或者在那部老克林特·伊斯特伍德的电影《不可饶恕》里，赢得枪战的人是那个不退缩的人。他装枪和射击时保持冷静，而不是到处乱跑。所以我认为我们通过焦虑浪费了太多能量，如果你能保持冷静并继续做事，那是一种超能力。我最近自己意识到了这一点，当时我在商业上遇到一个冲突局面。那是一个高度冲突的局面，很不幸。我想我们已经解决了。但在经历这个高度冲突的过程中，我几年前经历过类似的情况，那时我焦虑得多。我记得那段时间，记得我有多紧张，多害怕，经历了恐惧和愤怒的波动，整个人一直很激动，紧张，睡不好。但这次我异常冷静，甚至有点享受，因为就像在练习我的手艺。当然，现在更容易做到，因为我更有钱了，但与此同时，它并没有困扰我。它非常机械。正因为如此，我可以非常高效地处理它，同时还能做很多其他事情。我的大脑没有一直像漩涡一样旋转，处理 10 个不同的问题，整天基于恐惧地胡思乱想。这些事情大多数永远不会发生。所以我确实相信，保持冷静并继续做事是一种超能力。当然，如果焦虑是你唯一的动力，那你就有问题了。但我认为纯粹的动力并非来自焦虑。

turns.en: Let's talk about attribution here. What would you attribute the—and I know it's difficult to isolate variables—but if let's just say you have a family, so let's just say one of your kids comes to you and says, 'Dad, I'm suffering from a lot of anxiety. What should I do?' Or you observe it and you want to help your kid out. What types of recommendations would you make or might you make? Another question if you prefer it: what has helped you to go from the...
turns.zh: 我们来谈谈归因。你会把什么归因于——我知道很难隔离变量——但假设你有一个家庭，假设你的一个孩子来找你说：‘爸爸，我很焦虑。我该怎么办？’或者你观察到并想帮助你的孩子。你会给出什么样的建议？或者如果你更喜欢另一个问题：是什么帮助你从……

turns.en: It's really hard to separate all the pieces out. I mean, it comes from a combination of philosophy, yoga, meditation, getting older, having kids. You know, like you, I've had some psychedelic experiences, but those are very far back in the past, just a distant memory at this point. But I would say the number one thing that has been very, very important for me is meditation. And it's a stupid thing to say because it's so trite, everybody just says it now. But when I say meditation, I don't mean sitting there and watching your breath or chanting a mantra. I mean self-examination. And meditation is a great way to do that self-therapy. It's sitting there with your thoughts. So anxiety, this pervasive non-specific anxiety, we're just constantly on edge about everything, that comes from an unexamined life. I think Socrates said something like 'An unexamined life is not worth living.' I forget who said that quote, something like that. But it's correct. It's your unexamined life that is causing the problems. And you can examine it in multiple ways. You can have some crazy mushroom trip where it all comes out one night. You could do a lot of meditation, sitting there with yourself and letting your mind run crazy, and then seeing what's actually in your mind that your mind wants to tell you and have you listen to and have you resolve that is unresolved. It could be through therapy, it could be through reading lots of philosophy and reflection and long walks. So there are many, many ways to tackle it. But it's that spending that time with yourself to examine why you are having these thoughts. Think about it this way: we spend so much time in our relationships—our relationship with our wives, our relationship with our colleagues, our relationship with our business partners, our relationship with our friends. The most important relationship you have is with yourself. It's with this voice in your head that is constantly rattling every waking hour. It's this crazy roommate living inside your mind who's always chattering, always chattering, never shuts up. And you can't control these thoughts; they just come up out of you, you don't even know where from. That quality of those conversations you're having in your head all the time—that is your world. That is the world you live in. That's the worldview you have, that's the lenses you see through. And that's going to determine the quality of your life more than anything else. And if you want to see what the quality of your life actually is, put down the drink, put down the computer, put down the smartphone, put down the book, put down the headphones. Just sit by yourself doing nothing. And then you will know what the quality of your life actually is, because that's what you're always running away from. That's why people, when they try to meditate, sit down and say 'I hate it, I can't sit still.' Why? Because your mind is eating you alive. Your life is unexamined. Your mind is running in loops over things that it has not resolved. And because they're not resolved, when you run around in your normal life, it's not that those problems have gone away; they're just there. They're there, but they're provoking anxiety. And what you think of as the anxiety that's kind of consuming you and you can't identify the source—that's just the tip of an iceberg poking out from underneath the water. And underneath is this giant pile of garbage: decisions that were made without too much thought, situations that you're in that you haven't resolved, problems that you have or desires that you have that have gone unmet or unmanifested, contradictions that you're living in, or ways in which you feel trapped. So proper meditation, proper examination, should ruin the life that you're currently living. It should cause you to leave relationships, it should cause you to re-establish boundaries with family members, with colleagues, it should cause you to quit your job, it should cause you to change your eating patterns, it should cause you to spend more time with yourself, it should cause you to change what books you read, it should cause you to change who your friends are. If it doesn't do that, it's not real examination. If it doesn't come attached with destruction of your current life, then you can't create the new life in which you will not have the anxiety—or at least parts of it, not necessarily whole cloth.
turns.zh: 真的很难把所有东西分开。它来自哲学、瑜伽、冥想、变老、有孩子等等的组合。像你一样，我也有过一些迷幻体验，但那都是很久以前的事了，现在只是遥远的记忆。但我要说，对我而言最重要的一件事就是冥想。这说起来很傻，因为太老套了，现在每个人都在说。但我说冥想，不是指坐在那里观察呼吸或念咒语。我指的是自我审视。冥想是进行自我疗愈的好方法。就是和你的想法待在一起。焦虑，这种无处不在的非特定焦虑，我们总是对一切感到紧张，这来自于未经审视的生活。我记得苏格拉底说过类似‘未经审视的生活不值得过’的话。我忘了是谁说的，大概是那样。但这是对的。正是你未经审视的生活导致了问题。你可以通过多种方式审视它。你可以有一次疯狂的蘑菇之旅，一夜之间全部爆发。你可以做很多冥想，和自己待在一起，让思绪疯狂，然后看看你的内心到底有什么，你的内心想告诉你什么，想让你倾听并解决那些未解决的问题。也可以通过治疗，通过阅读大量哲学著作和反思，通过长时间的散步。有很多很多方法可以解决。但关键是要花时间与自己相处，审视为什么你会有这些想法。这样想：我们在人际关系上花了那么多时间——与妻子的关系，与同事的关系，与商业伙伴的关系，与朋友的关系。但最重要的关系是你和自己的关系。是和你脑海中那个在每个清醒时刻都喋喋不休的声音的关系。那是你内心中的一个疯狂室友，总是喋喋不休，从不闭嘴。你无法控制这些想法；它们就这样冒出来，你甚至不知道它们从何而来。那些你脑海中不断进行的对话的质量——那就是你的世界。那是你生活的世界。那是你的世界观，是你透过它看世界的镜头。而这将比任何其他东西都更能决定你生活的质量。如果你想知道你生活的真实质量，放下酒杯，放下电脑，放下智能手机，放下书，放下耳机。就自己坐着，什么都不做。然后你就会知道你生活的真实质量，因为那正是你一直在逃避的东西。这就是为什么人们尝试冥想时，坐下来会说‘我讨厌它，我坐不住。’为什么？因为你的内心在吞噬你。你的生活未经审视。你的思绪在未解决的事情上循环。因为它们没有解决，当你在日常生活中忙碌时，并不是那些问题消失了；它们只是在那里。它们在那里，但它们在引发焦虑。而你认为那种消耗你、你找不到来源的焦虑——那只是冰山一角，露出水面的一小部分。下面是一大堆垃圾：未经深思熟虑做出的决定，你尚未解决的处境，你未满足或未实现的欲望，你生活中的矛盾，或者你感到被困住的方式。所以，真正的冥想，真正的审视，应该摧毁你目前的生活。它应该让你离开某些关系，它应该让你与家人、同事重新建立边界，它应该让你辞职，它应该让你改变饮食习惯，它应该让你花更多时间与自己相处，它应该让你改变读的书，它应该让你改变你的朋友。如果它没有做到这些，那就不是真正的审视。如果它不伴随着对当前生活的破坏，那么你就无法创造没有焦虑的新生活——至少是部分没有，不一定是全部。

turns.en: So let's dive into meditation. Because as you mentioned, it's a term that is used a lot, and in the minds of most, it is represented by things that you just said you do not do, like the mantra-based meditation or following the breath. And you and I have meditated before. I don't know if your approach is still similar, but it is quite a—I'd hate to talk about trite paradigm shift in a sense, at least in the way that I experienced it alongside you at one point, which was literally doing nothing for extended periods of time. I don't know if that's still the case, but could you describe the practice a bit? Because it was such a burden lifted in a sense when the pass/fail is removed. And the experience that I had after say a week of doing this twice a day—which is not necessarily what people do in normal life—but it was pretty profound. So could you describe the current practice, which may be different from what I experienced?
turns.zh: 那么让我们深入探讨冥想。因为正如你提到的，这个词被广泛使用，在大多数人心中，它代表的是你刚才说你不做的事情，比如基于咒语的冥想或跟随呼吸。你和我以前一起冥想过。我不知道你的方法是否还类似，但这是一种——我不想说陈词滥调的范式转变，至少在我曾经和你一起体验的方式中，就是长时间什么都不做。我不知道现在是否还是这样，但你能描述一下这个练习吗？因为当通过/失败的标准被移除时，在某种意义上是一种负担的解脱。我每天做两次，坚持一周后的体验——虽然这不一定是人们在正常生活中会做的——但相当深刻。所以你能描述一下你现在的练习吗？可能和我体验过的不同。

turns.en: Yeah, I actually have two different things that I do. And I almost hate calling it meditation because everyone has in their mind a preconceived notion of what meditation is. Let's not even call it that. I would say that there are two self-examination practices that I do. And I don't even call them practices anymore, because sometimes I do them because I feel like them and I enjoy them, or because it feels right, and sometimes I don't. Because anything done routinely sort of becomes its own trap and is not going to get you anywhere. It just becomes like another spiritual high, another checkbox.
turns.zh: 是的，我实际上做两件不同的事情。我几乎讨厌称之为冥想，因为每个人心中都有对冥想的先入为主的观念。我们甚至不这么叫它。我会说我有两种自我审视的练习。我甚至不再称它们为练习，因为有时我做它们是因为我想做，我喜欢它们，或者因为感觉对了，有时我不做。因为任何例行公事的事情都会变成它自己的陷阱，不会让你有任何进展。它只会变成另一种精神高潮，另一个复选框。

turns.en: Is it fair though to say that you're able to opt in and out now because you had a certain front-loading period doing it consistently?
turns.zh: 但这样说公平吗：你现在能够选择做或不做，是因为你之前有一段持续做的前置期？

turns.en: Actually, I'll say there are three different things that I do, and I'll go through them. The first is I just read a lot of philosophy, especially at nighttime before I go to bed. Which ones? Schopenhauer, Maxims? Who are we talking about? Yeah, Schopenhauer. Western philosophy is my current favorite, although I've definitely moved around on that one. Eastern philosophy, I'll read everything from Osho—I know he's discredited and been canceled, but fantastic, that makes me like him even more. Krishnamurti, I don't know, Kapil Gupta, Rupert Spira, I mean it's all over the place. Anthony de Mello, he's fantastic. Actually, I'm gonna start with one book: start with Anthony de Mello's.
turns.zh: 实际上，我会说我有三件不同的事情要做，我来逐一说明。第一件是我大量阅读哲学，尤其是在晚上睡觉前。哪些？叔本华，《箴言》？我们在说谁？对，叔本华。西方哲学是我目前的最爱，虽然我在这方面也换过很多。东方哲学，我读奥修的所有东西——我知道他被质疑、被取消，但很精彩，这让我更喜欢他。克里希那穆提，我不知道，卡皮尔·古普塔，鲁伯特·斯皮拉，我的意思是五花八门。安东尼·德·梅洛，他很棒。实际上，我要从一本书开始：从安东尼·德·梅洛的《觉醒》开始。

turns.en: Way to Love, or his book Awareness, they're both really good. But there's a ton of them. I basically read a lot of philosophers: Siddhartha, Vasistha's Yoga, Bhagavad Gita, the Daodejing. You know, I'm always going through one of these books at any given time, and usually rereading for inspiration. I'll read these at night. Usually there'll be one or two things I'll catch on to, I'll reflect on it before I go to sleep. So that is a form of self-examination. And it's not done because it's a formula; it's done because I'm genuinely interested in these things. So it's not work to me, it's fun.
turns.zh: 《爱的方式》或者他的《觉知》那本书，都很好。但还有很多。我基本上读很多哲学家的书：悉达多、瓦西斯塔瑜伽、薄伽梵歌、道德经。你知道，我随时都在读这些书中的一本，而且通常是重读以获取灵感。我晚上读这些书。通常我会抓住一两个点，在睡前反思。所以这是一种自我审视。这不是因为某种公式而做，而是因为我真的对这些东西感兴趣。所以对我来说不是工作，是乐趣。

turns.en: The second thing I do, which I've been doing for a long time now, is just trying to be aware of my thoughts. Not in a 'sit there and be like, oh, I'm gonna be aware of my thoughts' kind of way, but just realizing that a lot of these thoughts that come up aren't unbidden. I don't control them. It's not like I decided to have this thought. I don't even know what I'm saying to you right now; it's coming out of my mouth at the same time as I'm thinking it. So where is this coming from? Who is this person? What is this person saying? Is this true? Is it correct? Has it been correct in the past? Is he just being paranoid now? Is he being crazy? What are his underlying motivations? And I'm not even questioning so much, but more just kind of seeing it. And after a while, when you see it, you start seeing through your own BS. You start seeing how you're mainly just justifying whatever the heck you want to believe because it's good for your survival, it's good for your replication, or it's good for your money, or it's going to get you laid, or any of these various things.
turns.zh: 我做的第二件事，我已经做了很久了，就是试着觉察我的念头。不是那种‘坐在那里，哦，我要觉察我的念头’的方式，而是意识到很多冒出来的念头并不是不请自来的。我控制不了它们。并不是我决定要有这个念头。我甚至不知道我现在在跟你说什么；它从我嘴里说出来，同时我也在想它。所以这从哪来的？这个人是谁？这个人在说什么？这是真的吗？正确吗？过去正确过吗？他现在只是偏执吗？他疯了吗？他潜在的动机是什么？我甚至不是在质疑那么多，而更多是看着它。过了一段时间，当你看着它，你开始看穿你自己的胡扯。你开始看到你主要只是在为你想要相信的任何东西辩护，因为它有利于你的生存，有利于你的繁衍，或者有利于你的钱，或者能让你上床，或者各种这类东西。

turns.en: And when you're doing that reflection, is it sitting at a table with pen and paper? Is it half lotus with your eyes closed? What is it? No, it's literally just walking around. It's just walking around. It's nice. It's just life. Just if you say something to me, I'm always going to listen to it with a slightly critical filter because you're external to me. Applying that same filter to my own thoughts gives me a level of peace and distance from them, and the ability to see through my own BS, which I think makes my life better. So I've just found that to be a useful way of life. But there are lots of times where I forget to do it. I get caught up in some emotions, some runaway train of thought. But usually these days I can catch myself and be like, 'Oh okay, I'm in that mindset again. I'm having that mode of reactions again.' And when the mind sort of sees me chattering, it quiets down a bit. Right? And like I said, it's back to that crazy roommate analogy, which I originally picked up from Michael Singer, by the way. He has a good book called The Untethered Soul. But you know, the crazy roommate analogy: like you would eventually tell your roommate to shut up, or you would tell them to start justifying their claims, or you would at least at some level start dismissing them for just kind of rambling all the time in this fear paranoia mode. So I think just kind of viewing your thoughts as sort of these unbidden things that arise from within you but aren't really necessarily the whole story, and should be viewed with the same level of skepticism that you would apply to an outside observer. I think that framework is helpful. And if you find it to be true, then you'll find yourself doing it more often. If you find it not to be true, if you're like, 'No, actually I choose these thoughts and I want to have them and that's who I am and this all makes sense to me,' then you can stop viewing it with a critical eye. That's up to you.
turns.zh: 当你做这种反思时，是坐在桌前用纸笔吗？是半莲花坐闭着眼睛吗？是什么？不，其实就是到处走走。就是到处走走。很好。就是生活。如果你对我说什么，我总是会带着一点批判的滤镜去听，因为你是外在于我的。把同样的滤镜用在我自己的念头上，让我对它们有了一种平静和距离感，以及看穿自己胡扯的能力，我觉得这让我的生活更好了。所以我发现这是一个有用的生活方式。但很多时候我会忘记做。我会陷入一些情绪，一些失控的思绪列车。但通常现在我能抓住自己，然后说，‘哦好吧，我又进入那种心态了。我又在那种反应模式了。’当头脑看到我在喋喋不休时，它会安静一点。对吧？就像我说的，这又回到了那个疯狂室友的类比，顺便说，我最初是从迈克尔·辛格那里学来的。他有一本好书叫《不羁的灵魂》。但你知道，疯狂室友的类比：就像你最终会让你的室友闭嘴，或者你会让他们开始为自己的说法辩护，或者至少在一定程度上你会开始无视他们，因为他们总是处于这种恐惧偏执的模式中喋喋不休。所以我认为把你的念头看作这些不请自来的东西，它们从你内心升起，但不一定是全部真相，应该用你对外部观察者同样的怀疑态度来看待。我认为这个框架很有帮助。如果你发现它是真的，那么你会发现自己更经常这样做。如果你发现它不是真的，如果你觉得，‘不，实际上我选择了这些念头，我想要它们，这就是我，这一切对我来说都有道理，’那么你可以停止用批判的眼光看待它。这取决于你。

turns.en: The last one is the one that I think people think of as more traditionally meditation, which is sitting down and meditating. On that one, I learned from an Indian gentleman, same character you learned from, who had a very simple system. And I just found it the most compelling form of meditation I've ever tried. Which is: you sit there for 60 minutes. So unfortunately, not less than an hour at a time, because it takes 30 to 40 minutes to sink in past the initial chattering, so you get to the good part, or the so-called runner's high equivalent. And you sit for 60 minutes every day, and you do it for at least 60 days. And you do it first thing in the morning, when your mind is clear and you're alert and you've had a good night's sleep. And you sit up with your back straight, and you can use cushions or you can use a chair or whatever. There's no magic position. And just whatever happens happens. Whatever your mind wants to do, you just let it do. If it wants to talk, you let it talk. If it wants to fight, you let it fight. If it wants to be quiet, you let it be quiet. If it wants to chant a mantra or pay attention to breathing, you can do that. But you don't force anything. You just kind of let it happen. And so you don't fight it, you don't resist it, you don't argue with it, you don't double down on it. You just kind of let things happen. And when you do that for at least 60 days, my experience has been that you kind of clear out your mental inbox. And all the craziness that was going on, all the chattering, will come out. Some problems will get resolved. You will have some epiphanies. You will make changes to your life. Some will be self-examination. Some of it just needs to be heard once and then it goes away. And eventually you'll get to a mental state of inbox zero, where now you're just thinking about what happened yesterday, you're kind of caught up, and your mind is relatively clear. And just your anxiety level goes down. You're living more peacefully.
turns.zh: 最后一种是我认为人们更传统地认为是冥想的方式，那就是坐下来冥想。关于这个，我从一位印度绅士那里学到的，就是你从同一个人那里学的，他有一个非常简单的系统。我发现这是我试过的最有说服力的冥想形式。那就是：你坐在那里 60 分钟。所以不幸的是，一次不能少于一个小时，因为需要 30 到 40 分钟才能沉下去，越过最初的喋喋不休，这样你才能进入好的部分，或者所谓的跑步者高潮的等价物。你每天坐 60 分钟，至少坚持 60 天。而且你早上第一件事就做，那时你的头脑清醒，警觉，而且睡了一晚好觉。你坐直，背挺直，可以用垫子或椅子什么的。没有神奇的姿势。然后发生什么就是什么。你的头脑想做什么，你就让它做。如果它想说话，你就让它说。如果它想打架，你就让它打。如果它想安静，你就让它安静。如果它想念咒语或注意呼吸，你也可以那样做。但你不强迫任何事。你就让它发生。所以你不与它斗争，不抵抗它，不争论，不加倍投入。你就让事情发生。当你这样做至少 60 天，我的经验是你会清空你的精神收件箱。所有那些疯狂的事情，所有的喋喋不休，都会出来。一些问题会得到解决。你会有一些顿悟。你会对你的生活做出改变。有些是自我审视。有些只需要被听到一次，然后就消失了。最终你会达到一种精神收件箱为零的状态，那时你只是在想昨天发生了什么，你有点跟上了，你的头脑相对清晰。你的焦虑水平下降了。你活得更平静了。

turns.en: I've been doing this for about two and a half years now, and I probably missed about a dozen days total. But there are some days where I've done two hours a day or more. And I will tell you that is the single most important thing that I do. It is a sheer joy. Much of it is highly entertaining, pleasurable. Sometimes it's just flat, it's nothingness. I can't even tell you why I do it. I can't even tell you what's going on in that state. But I will tell you that that time spent by myself is the most important time that I have. And thanks to that, I am now much more self-contained. I don't feel like I need other people. I don't need external sources of pleasure or happiness all the time. I drink less. I'm not attracted to trying any drugs whatsoever. It's just life is easier, it's more pleasant. I don't take things as seriously. I'm not afraid of my mortality as much anymore. I don't fear aging. I don't lust after things. I don't have this constant pervasive need to find something outside of me to make my life better. When the best hour of my day is spent by myself, then the world has very little to offer me. And I can still participate in it, but it doesn't have that grip on me that it used to. I don't fear solitary confinement. And I think that is a superpower. And I think everyone should have it. Everyone does have it. It's easy. It requires doing nothing. It's your birthright. You can't fail at it. There's no...
turns.zh: 我已经这样做了大约两年半了，总共可能错过了大约十二天。但有些日子我每天做两个小时或更多。我会告诉你这是我做的最重要的一件事。这是一种纯粹的快乐。很多时候它非常有趣，令人愉悦。有时它很平淡，是虚无。我甚至无法告诉你我为什么做。我甚至无法告诉你那种状态里发生了什么。但我会告诉你，那些独处的时间是我拥有的最重要的时间。多亏了它，我现在更加自足。我不觉得我需要别人。我不需要总是从外部获得快乐或幸福。我喝得更少了。我对尝试任何毒品都不感兴趣。生活更轻松，更愉快。我不再把事情看得那么严重。我不再那么害怕死亡。我不害怕衰老。我不渴望东西。我没有那种持续不断的、无处不在的需求，要去外面找什么东西让我的生活更好。当我一天中最好的时光是独自度过时，世界能给我的就很少了。我仍然可以参与其中，但它不再像以前那样抓住我。我不害怕单独监禁。我认为这是一种超能力。我认为每个人都应该拥有它。每个人都拥有它。它很容易。它不需要做任何事。这是你与生俱来的权利。你不可能失败。没有……

turns.en: There's no way to fail at it. Literally all you have to do is just sit down and close your eyes and just be, give yourself a break for an hour every day, just take the time off from the world.
turns.zh: 这件事不可能失败。你只需要坐下来，闭上眼睛，放空自己，每天给自己一小时的休息，从世界中抽离出来。

turns.en: A few follow-ups. Krishnamurti, do you have any favorites of his or anything that you would suggest someone start with if they have no familiarity with Krishnamurti? And I'll second Anthony De Mello also.
turns.zh: 几个后续问题。克里希那穆提，你有他喜欢的作品或建议初学者从哪里开始吗？我也推荐安东尼·德·梅洛。

turns.en: Yeah, I actually remember starting with Krishna. He's a tough one, but if you're going to start somewhere, I started with this book called Think on These Things. There's also The Book of Life. He's very cerebral, very intellectual, and a little confusing, but he definitely speaks truth and he walks the walk. Anthony De Mello's The Way to Love, Osho's book The Great Challenge. And I know people think Osho is a fake guru and all that, but he had some real stuff too. He's very articulate, he understood a lot. There's a great clip from him on SoundCloud that says, it's called 'Life Has No Goals, No Purpose.' I listen to that one a lot. There's a lot of them out there. Once you start going down this rabbit hole, you'll find them. The classics: the Bhagavad Gita, get a good translation. Stephen Miller, I think, has a good translation. He also has a good translation of the Tao Te Ching. Those are both really interesting. Again, only if it appeals to you. I mean, there's ancient wisdom. In Schopenhauer, read Councils and Maxims. You know, these are smart people self-reflecting. But overall, the point is not to read, the point is to inspire so that you yourself can reflect. And Kappa Gupta is an account on Twitter, he has a couple of great books. Direct Truth is a fantastic book, written very recently and pulls no punches. But you know, all of these are basically there to inspire you to self-reflect. And if you're not interested in self-reflection, if you're not at that stage in life, then don't do it. It's a waste of time. In fact, I would say the reason to do these things is just because they make your life better, because they make you more effective, because they have some utility to you. If they do not have utility to you, if they don't make your life better, then drop it. It's useless.
turns.zh: 是的，我确实是从克里希那穆提开始的。他很难懂，但如果你要开始，我从一本叫《从已知中解脱》的书开始的。还有《生命之书》。他非常理智、非常知性，有点令人困惑，但他确实说真话，并且言行一致。安东尼·德·梅洛的《爱的道路》，奥修的书《伟大的挑战》。我知道有人认为奥修是假大师之类的，但他也有一些真东西。他很有口才，理解了很多。SoundCloud 上有一段他的精彩片段，叫《生命没有目标，没有目的》。我经常听。还有很多这样的资源。一旦你开始深入这个兔子洞，你就会找到它们。经典作品：《薄伽梵歌》，找个好译本。斯蒂芬·米勒，我想他有个好译本。他也有《道德经》的好译本。这两本都很有趣。再说一次，只有当你感兴趣时才读。我的意思是，里面有古老的智慧。叔本华的《人生的智慧》。你知道，这些都是聪明人的自我反思。但总的来说，重点不是阅读，重点是激发你自己去反思。还有 Twitter 上的 Kappa Gupta，他有两本很棒的书。《直接真理》是一本很棒的书，最近写的，毫不留情。但你知道，所有这些基本上都是为了激发你自我反思。如果你对自我反思不感兴趣，如果你还没到那个阶段，那就别做。那是浪费时间。事实上，我认为做这些事情的原因只是它们能让你的生活更好，让你更有效率，对你有用。如果它们对你没用，不能改善你的生活，那就放弃。它毫无用处。

turns.en: I will also second the reading of philosophy. And this is not for kind of idle or abstract like semantic tail-chasing, right? It's for whatever reason on a very pragmatic level, deeply soothing to read the insights and observations of those who are considering things that are not of today, they're not of this week, they're not of this month. They are for much broader periods of time or timeless in some capacity, right? It alleviates some of the manufactured emergency and urgency that is upon us by every possible input.
turns.zh: 我也推荐阅读哲学。这不是为了那种无聊或抽象的语义追逐，对吧？而是出于某种原因，在非常务实的层面上，阅读那些思考非今日、非本周、非本月之事的人的见解和观察，会让人深感安慰。它们涉及更广阔的时期，或在某种程度上是永恒的，对吧？它缓解了我们被各种输入强加的、人为制造的紧急和紧迫感。

turns.en: Oh yeah, the news and Twitter are so toxic. The human brain is not designed to manage every emergency in the world in real time. We're not designed to process all the breaking news on the planet in our heads. And this is one of my recent tweets, but the goal of the media these days is to make every problem your problem. That's how they get attention, it's how they get clicks. You know, you have tribal wars going on, zombies battling each other, and they want to pull you in, where even non-participation is not allowed anymore. Neutral bystanders are not allowed. So it's a crazy world out there. There's a circus going on with the monkeys flinging feces at each other, and they want to drag you into the fight. Really, what you want to focus on is what's timeless. Timeless is great. You know, the old questions all have old answers, and they're best consumed from the old practitioners because they were very clear-minded about it. They weren't busy trying to fit into the politics of this time; they were fitting to the politics of that time, but that's lost to us now. So actually, another great source is I have your Dao Seneca series on Audible, and I listen to it on repeat when I'm working out a lot. So that's very kind. Yeah, those Letters to Lucilius, amazing, amazing stuff. And every time I go through it, I learn something new.
turns.zh: 哦，是的，新闻和 Twitter 太有毒了。人类大脑不是用来实时管理世界上每一个紧急事件的。我们不是用来在脑子里处理地球上所有突发新闻的。这是我最近的一条推文，但如今媒体的目标是把每个问题都变成你的问题。他们就是这样吸引注意力、获取点击量的。你知道，有部落战争在进行，僵尸互相争斗，他们想把你拉进去，连不参与都不被允许了。中立旁观者不被允许。所以外面是个疯狂的世界。有一场马戏表演，猴子们互相扔粪便，他们想把你拖进战斗。真正你应该关注的是永恒的东西。永恒很棒。你知道，老问题都有老答案，最好从老实践者那里获取，因为他们对此头脑清晰。他们没有忙着适应这个时代的政治；他们适应的是那个时代的政治，但这对我们来说已经消失了。所以实际上，另一个很好的资源是，我在 Audible 上有你的《道·塞内卡》系列，我锻炼时经常反复听。那真是太好了。是的，那些《道德书简》，太棒了，太棒了。每次我读它，都能学到新东西。

turns.en: Yeah, the town of Seneca, the Moral Letters to Lucilius. If people want to look on public domain sources, you can find these letters, the Moral Letters to Lucilius, also spelled as you might read Lucilius, by Seneca. And so that's an audiobook series that I produced, and you can find the PDFs for free online as well.
turns.zh: 是的，塞内卡的《道德书简》。如果人们想在公共领域资源中查找，可以找到这些信件，塞内卡的《道德书简》，也可以拼写为 Lucilius。这是我制作的一个有声书系列，你也可以在网上免费找到 PDF 版本。

turns.en: Let's talk about some brass tacks stuff, since I think people will be very interested. You mentioned monkeys throwing feces, tribalism. In times like this, this is being recorded in 2020, first day of October. A lot of folks are interested in stores of value. They are fearful of what could happen and considering investments like gold or cryptocurrency.
