And I wanted you to explain one of your recent tweets, this is just from the last few days, and it is: 'Crypto stable coins: dash, choose between blow up risk, censorship risk, and fraud risk.' What are crypto stable coins and what does this tweet mean?
turns.zh: 我们来谈谈一些实际的东西，因为我想人们会非常感兴趣。你提到了猴子扔粪便、部落主义。在这样的时代，这是 2020 年 10 月 1 日录制的。很多人对价值储存感兴趣。他们担心可能发生的事情，并考虑投资黄金或加密货币。我想让你解释一下你最近的一条推文，就是最近几天的，内容是：“加密稳定币：dash，在爆雷风险、审查风险和欺诈风险之间选择。”什么是加密稳定币？这条推文是什么意思？

turns.en: Yeah, just backing up for a second. Like, I think cryptocurrencies are probably one of the greatest inventions in human history. And the reason why they're interesting is because if you look at the technology industry, technology plays in unregulated spaces. It's very hard for technology to change regulated spaces, as Uber and Postmates and companies like that find out. But generally, the reason technology works is because it creates its own frontier. It is a digital frontier that has been created. The physical frontiers are all closed, and the new world has been colonized, and the Wild West has been tamed. Where do you go to create new things free of interference and regulation and kind of exercise maximum creativity? And so that's been done mostly in the technology space. And one of the areas that has been protected by technological innovation from technological innovation is Wall Street, because they're, you know, they have regulatory capture, very bureaucratic. You know, the money industrial complex that runs a lot of our economy and runs a lot of Washington DC, where 20% of our GDP goes into financial engineering and into the Wall Street casino. And so cryptocurrencies are kind of how we get around that. And so they're sovereign resistant. They're designed to be completely decentralized. You don't need the violent power of the state to enforce the value of a cryptocurrency. And it allows for truly trustless transactions between humans without some king or authority or government or corporation having to be in the middle. And so it's very liberating to disconnect wealth creation, wealth storage, and wealth protection from the state. And that's what cryptocurrencies really enable.
turns.zh: 是的，先退一步。我认为加密货币可能是人类历史上最伟大的发明之一。它们有趣的原因在于，如果你看看科技行业，科技是在不受监管的空间中运作的。科技很难改变受监管的空间，正如 Uber 和 Postmates 等公司发现的那样。但一般来说，科技之所以有效，是因为它创造了自己的前沿。这是一个被创造出来的数字前沿。物理前沿都已关闭，新世界已被殖民，狂野西部已被驯服。你去哪里创造不受干扰和监管的新事物，并发挥最大的创造力？这主要在科技领域完成。而一个受到技术创新保护、免受技术创新影响的领域是华尔街，因为他们有监管俘获，非常官僚。你知道，那个运行我们大部分经济和华盛顿特区的货币工业复合体，我们 GDP 的 20%用于金融工程和华尔街赌场。所以加密货币是我们绕过这一点的方式。因此，它们具有主权抵抗力。它们被设计成完全去中心化。你不需要国家的暴力力量来强制执行加密货币的价值。它允许人与人之间真正无需信任的交易，而无需任何国王、权威、政府或公司居中。因此，将财富创造、财富储存和财富保护与国家脱钩是非常解放的。这就是加密货币真正实现的东西。

turns.en: One second, let me just pause to say that at one point you and I did a very thorough cryptocurrency 101 conversation with a domain super expert named Nick Szabo, S-Z-A-B-O, which people can also find for more on kind of the history and basics of some of the crypto.
turns.zh: 等一下，让我暂停一下，说一句，有一次你和我与一位领域超级专家 Nick Szabo（S-Z-A-B-O）进行了一次非常深入的加密货币 101 对话，人们也可以找到它，了解更多关于加密货币的历史和基础知识。

turns.en: Yeah, Nick is a bonafide genius and a pioneer in the space.
turns.zh: 是的，Nick 是真正的天才，是这个领域的先驱。

turns.en: He also has a great blog called Unenumerated that I highly recommend. But anyway, the best known cryptocurrency is Bitcoin, and Bitcoin is trying to be the new gold or the new Swiss bank account all rolled into one. It has some advantages as a store of value: it can be stored digitally, so it's hard to seize; it's very easy to verify technologically, unlike gold; it's easy to send across national boundaries electronically over the internet; it's easily divisible and verifiable down to its authenticity, where gold is hard to do; it can be divided down to millions and trillions of a Bitcoin very easily; it is somewhat programmable, so you can put it inside smart contracts and do some intelligent things with it. So it has a lot going for it as a store of value.
turns.zh: 他还有一个很棒的博客叫 Unenumerated，我强烈推荐。总之，最知名的加密货币是比特币，比特币试图成为新黄金或新瑞士银行账户的结合体。作为价值储存手段，它有一些优势：可以数字存储，因此难以被没收；技术上非常容易验证，不像黄金；可以轻松通过互联网跨国界电子传输；易于分割和验证真伪，而黄金很难做到；可以非常容易地分割到百万分之一甚至万亿分之一比特币；它具有一定可编程性，因此可以放入智能合约中，做一些智能的事情。所以作为价值储存手段，它有很多优点。

turns.en: What's difficult about it is it's not really private. Although there are parts of it that are private, it's not completely private. And it's untrusted; it's brand new as far as stores of value go, only from 2009. So people don't know if it'll be around forever, and how dependent it is on the internet. People aren't really certain about it. It's not like the proof-of-work mining algorithm that makes it possible, and can it be hijacked, can it be centralized, can it have a bug, can it break? Every year that Bitcoin survives and goes through one of the various challenges facing it, it gets more valuable as people entrust it a little bit more. But it is extremely volatile. You buy Bitcoin, you're buying a very speculative asset at the moment, and you're speculating that it will become digital gold or possibly even all store of value, and so be incredibly valuable. But along the way, there are lots of hiccups. At any point it could break, it could go to zero.
turns.zh: 它的困难之处在于它并不真正隐私。虽然有些部分是私密的，但并非完全隐私。而且它不受信任；作为价值储存手段，它是全新的，仅始于 2009 年。所以人们不知道它是否会永远存在，以及它在多大程度上依赖互联网。人们对此并不确定。它不像工作量证明挖矿算法那样使其成为可能，而且它是否会被劫持、被中心化、有漏洞、崩溃？每一年比特币存活下来并经历各种挑战，随着人们越来越信任它，它变得更有价值。但它极其波动。你买入比特币，你目前买入的是一种高度投机的资产，你是在投机它会成为数字黄金，甚至可能成为所有价值储存手段，因此变得极其有价值。但在此过程中，有很多障碍。任何时候它都可能崩溃，可能归零。

ts.sec: Decentralized Finance as a New Casino

turns.en: A lot of people who are now participating in the crypto world are building a decentralized Wall Street. They call it DeFi, for decentralized finance. But I actually think it's more like 'defy' — just defy the government, defy. So I think we're seeing a whole new casino that's better than Wall Street spring up. And decentralized finance, unlike Wall Street, this one is 24/7 open, it's 365 days a year, it's available around the world to everybody. It's trustless in that there's math and algorithms and code underneath, not Goldman Sachs front-running your trades or whatever. Not that I'm saying they do, but there's flash boys and all that kind of craziness that goes on. And you can program it to do things and to make bets or hedges or calls that you just can't even do on Wall Street. So I think we're building a better Wall Street using cryptocurrencies.
turns.zh: 很多现在参与加密世界的人正在构建一个去中心化的华尔街。他们称之为 DeFi，即去中心化金融。但我实际上认为这更像是‘反抗’——就是反抗政府，反抗。所以我认为我们看到一个比华尔街更好的全新赌场正在兴起。而去中心化金融与华尔街不同，它是 24/7 开放的，一年 365 天，全世界每个人都可以使用。它是无需信任的，因为底层是数学、算法和代码，而不是高盛抢先交易你的订单之类的。我不是说他们这么做，但确实有‘闪电小子’之类的疯狂事情发生。而且你可以编程让它做事情，进行华尔街甚至无法做到的押注、对冲或看涨。所以我认为我们正在用加密货币建造一个更好的华尔街。

turns.en: Everyone who participates in that doesn't necessarily want to take on the volatility of these cryptocurrencies. So we've created these things called stablecoins, which track the value of the US dollar, so that when you're trading, for example, you can still do it in dollar-denominated terms. This is a tricky thing because the moment you're now trying to track some asset in the real world like dollars, then you have to basically peg the value somehow to something that exists in the real world. And cryptocurrencies do best when everything is on the blockchain, when everything is electronic and digital and controlled by computers in the cloud. So these stablecoins have come along that mimic the value of the US dollar. The best known ones are Tether, USDC which is Coinbase's coin, and Maker or DAI which is an algorithmically stable coin.
turns.zh: 每个参与其中的人不一定都想承担这些加密货币的波动性。所以我们创造了所谓的稳定币，它们追踪美元的价值，这样当你交易时，例如，你仍然可以用美元计价。这是一件棘手的事情，因为一旦你试图追踪现实世界中的某种资产（比如美元），那么你基本上必须将价值与现实中存在的东西挂钩。而加密货币在一切都上链、一切都电子化数字化并由云端计算机控制时表现最好。所以这些稳定币出现了，它们模仿美元的价值。最知名的是 Tether、Coinbase 的 USDC，以及 Maker 或 DAI，这是一种算法稳定币。

turns.en: Is there no free lunch?
turns.zh: 没有免费的午餐吗？

turns.en: Well, is this something people should think of? What would be a comparable that people might be more familiar with? It's not an index, it's like an ETF? Or how would you think about it? Is there another digital dollar? These are designed so that you can live in crypto land. This is living on a blockchain, it's math-based code, but really your value is pegged to dollars, so it's not going up or down with Bitcoin. It's just worth a dollar all the time. So if you want to hold a dollar in crypto land, the way you do it is by buying one of these so-called stablecoins. But all my tweet was observing is that there's no free lunch. What you're actually doing underneath is you have crypto, and crypto is incredibly volatile, and you're trying to convert a volatile asset into a stable asset, so there has to be a cost for that.
turns.zh: 嗯，这是人们应该考虑的吗？有什么人们更熟悉的类似物吗？它不是指数，像 ETF？或者你怎么看？有另一种数字美元吗？这些稳定币的设计是为了让你可以生活在加密世界。这是生活在区块链上，基于数学的代码，但你的价值实际上与美元挂钩，所以它不会随比特币涨跌。它始终只值一美元。所以如果你想在加密世界持有美元，方法就是购买这些所谓的稳定币之一。但我那条推文只是观察到没有免费的午餐。你实际上在做的是，你拥有加密货币，而加密货币极其波动，你试图将波动资产转换为稳定资产，所以这必然有成本。

turns.en: What is that called? Like the subprime mortgage crisis?
turns.zh: 那叫什么？像次贷危机？

turns.en: That's right, exactly. There's no free lunch. So what does that cost? And all I was saying is that the three main categories of stablecoins today, they each impose one or more of these costs. One cost is fraud risk, where people suspect this of Tether: like actually this thing says it's backed by dollars but it's not actually backed by dollars. I don't know if this company has dollars underneath, so you have to kind of take their word for it. So now you're trusting them. Meaning they would have actual physical dollars, like you would have physical gold? Correct. So for every Tether they issue, they claim to have a dollar somewhere, but what if they don't? And you just don't know. So you're trusting them, and there could be fraud underneath. I'm not saying there is, I have no evidence, but you're basically now back to the trusted third-party model that exists in just putting your money in the bank.
turns.zh: 没错，正是如此。没有免费的午餐。那么成本是什么？我只是说，今天三种主要类型的稳定币，每一种都施加了一种或多种成本。一种成本是欺诈风险，人们怀疑 Tether 就是这样：实际上这个东西声称由美元支持，但实际上并非如此。我不知道这家公司是否有美元支持，所以你只能相信他们的话。所以现在你在信任他们。意思是他们会有实际的物理美元，就像你拥有实物黄金一样？正确。所以他们每发行一个 Tether，就声称在某处有一美元，但如果没有呢？而你无从知晓。所以你信任他们，背后可能存在欺诈。我不是说一定有，我没有证据，但你基本上回到了信任第三方模式，就像把钱存在银行一样。

turns.en: The second kind is where you're dealing with someone like Coinbase or some company that is well known and trusted and is regulated, but then it's no different than holding normal dollars. There's censorship risk: if the government says 'hey, we don't like this character, seize their bank account,' Coinbase can turn off your USDC account, and your stablecoin has basically been seized. You're no longer decentralized and sovereign like you are with Bitcoin.
turns.zh: 第二种是你与像 Coinbase 这样知名、受信任且受监管的公司打交道，但这与持有普通美元没有区别。存在审查风险：如果政府说‘嘿，我们不喜欢这个人，没收他们的银行账户’，Coinbase 可以关闭你的 USDC 账户，你的稳定币基本上就被没收了。你不再像持有比特币那样去中心化和自主。

turns.en: The last kind of risk is a blow-up risk. Something like Maker: it's collateralized, but it's collateralized with Bitcoin and Ethereum. So what they're hoping is that the price of Bitcoin and Ethereum won't move so drastically that the peg breaks and you lose money on your so-called stablecoin. It's just an observation: there's no free lunch. So if you're trying to create this mythical stable currency out of thin air in what is inherently a very volatile space, you have to pay for it. And people think today they're not paying for it, they're just getting these free crypto stable dollars, or they're getting the stability aspect for free. But they're not. They're either taking on fraud risk, or censorship risk, or blow-up risk, or some combination of them.
turns.zh: 最后一种风险是崩盘风险。像 Maker 这样的：它有抵押品，但抵押品是比特币和以太坊。所以他们希望的是比特币和以太坊的价格不会剧烈波动，以至于脱钩，你会在所谓的稳定币上亏损。这只是一个观察：没有免费的午餐。所以如果你试图在一个本质上非常波动的空间中凭空创造出这种神话般的稳定货币，你必须为此付出代价。而人们今天认为他们没有付出代价，他们只是免费获得这些加密稳定美元，或者免费获得稳定性。但事实并非如此。他们要么承担欺诈风险，要么承担审查风险，要么承担崩盘风险，或者它们的某种组合。

ts.sec: Advice for Getting Started with Crypto

turns.en: So if you're advising — and this is not financial advice, this is just two friends talking obviously for informational purposes only — but if you're talking to a friend who has a fair amount of savings or investable capital and they say, 'Naval, I want to do something with crypto. How can I get started?'
turns.zh: 所以如果你在提供建议——这不是财务建议，只是两个朋友聊天，显然仅供信息参考——但如果你和一个有相当多储蓄或可投资资本的朋友聊天，他们说：‘Naval，我想做点加密货币相关的事。我该如何开始？’

turns.en: Should I get started? What do you say to them? Because there's such a broad spectrum of options. It's still, even though it's been simplified a lot, crypto is still quite confusing. It's very complicated.
turns.zh: 我应该开始吗？你怎么跟他们说？因为选择范围太广了。尽管已经简化了很多，但加密货币仍然相当令人困惑。它非常复杂。

turns.en: Absolutely. For people who are very smart, I would consider it's still quite confusing. Incredibly confusing. It's a domain of mathematicians, hackers, tech entrepreneurs, and a few people who really dig in. But it's still too hard to handle. It's dangerous. I can't hold my own crypto; I have to stick it inside funds and custodians because I'm a known public figure and it's a bearer asset. So ironically, I can't actually hold it.
turns.zh: 绝对如此。即使是非常聪明的人，我也认为它仍然相当令人困惑。极其令人困惑。这是一个数学家、黑客、科技企业家和少数真正深入研究的人的领域。但它仍然太难处理了。它很危险。我不能自己持有加密货币；我必须把它放在基金和托管人那里，因为我是知名公众人物，而且它是一种不记名资产。所以讽刺的是，我实际上不能持有它。

turns.en: You don't hold it because you would be...
turns.zh: 你不持有它是因为你会……

turns.en: Yeah, exactly. It's a bearer asset, so you don't want to bear a bearer asset. You have to put it inside vaults, the equivalent of Goldman Sachs in the space. So there are custodians like Anchorage, which is amazing, Coinbase, or CoinList, a company I helped start, who can hold on to your crypto assets for you. But that's not really the point of crypto. The point of crypto is to be your own bank. So if the government is coming to seize money or if they're printing lots of it, you can always withdraw your crypto assets from these organizations and carry it yourself. You can put it on your laptop, which is kind of crazy and risky and hard. You can do it through a hardware wallet like a Ledger or a Trezor, which are well-known hardware wallets where you have a small specific device designed just for carrying crypto. But you have to understand: you're replacing the banking system. You're literally replacing the government, all the guns and the police that back up the banking system. You're replacing all that and all the control that comes with it. So of course it's going to require some level of sophistication. You're literally building your own Swiss bank. So it is quite complicated.
turns.zh: 是的，没错。它是一种不记名资产，所以你不想要持有不记名资产。你必须把它放在金库里，相当于这个领域的 Goldman Sachs。所以有像 Anchorage（很棒）、Coinbase 或 CoinList（我帮助创立的公司）这样的托管人可以为你持有加密资产。但这并不是加密货币的真正意义。加密货币的意义在于成为你自己的银行。所以如果政府要来没收资金，或者他们大量印钞，你总是可以从这些组织中提取你的加密资产，自己携带。你可以把它放在笔记本电脑上，这有点疯狂、有风险且困难。你可以通过硬件钱包来做，比如 Ledger 或 Trezor，这些是知名的硬件钱包，有一个专门用于携带加密货币的小型特定设备。但你必须明白：你正在取代银行系统。你实际上是在取代政府，以及支持银行系统的所有枪支和警察。你正在取代所有这一切以及随之而来的所有控制。所以当然需要一定程度的 sophistication。你实际上是在建立你自己的瑞士银行。所以它确实相当复杂。

turns.en: Where to start?
turns.zh: 从哪里开始？

turns.en: Well, I don't like giving people buy signals because if you're going to be a good investor, you also have to have your own conviction. Then you'll know when to sell. If you call me and ask me when to buy, are you going to call me every day and say 'Should I sell? Should I sell?' No. You don't know that. It's really important. You have to fundamentally understand it yourself. So I would say the first thing is go down the crypto rabbit hole. Listen to the interview we do with Nick Szabo. Go online and start reading up on Bitcoin, read up on Ethereum, read up on the privacy coins like Zcash and Monero. Learn about the different high-level stuff in the crypto space. Decide how much exposure you want. Go buy it at a CoinList or a Coinbase or what have you. Figure out which custodian you want to use. Put it somewhere safe, probably in the cloud, unless you really know what you're doing. And if you really know what you're doing, then you can use one of the hardware wallets or one of the offline schemes. Make sure that it is protected in case you're attacked or kidnapped or someone comes to your house or steals your computer. You have to cover all those base cases. So it's not easy. But you're literally creating extra sovereign money. You're creating money where, if you ever have to flee the country, like the Jews had to leave Vienna back in the 1930s, you're not scrambling for gold. You're using crypto. If you're living in some country where they tend to seize all the money in your bank account, then you have unseizable money. If you're worried about hyperinflation as we print too many dollars, then you have a hedge against MMT and all the various excuses they're going to use to print money.
turns.zh: 嗯，我不喜欢给人们买入信号，因为如果你想成为一个好的投资者，你也必须有自己的信念。这样你才会知道何时卖出。如果你打电话问我什么时候买入，你会每天打电话问‘我应该卖吗？我应该卖吗？’不会的。你不知道。这非常重要。你必须自己从根本上理解它。所以我会说第一件事是深入加密货币的兔子洞。听我们与 Nick Szabo 的访谈。上网开始阅读关于比特币、以太坊、以及像 Zcash 和 Monero 这样的隐私币。了解加密领域不同的高级内容。决定你想要多少 exposure。去 CoinList 或 Coinbase 之类的地方购买。弄清楚你想用哪个托管人。把它放在安全的地方，可能是在云端，除非你真的知道自己在做什么。如果你真的知道自己在做什么，那么你可以使用硬件钱包或离线方案之一。确保它受到保护，以防你被攻击、绑架、有人闯入你家或偷走你的电脑。你必须覆盖所有这些基本情况。所以这并不容易。但你实际上是在创造额外的自主货币。你在创造一种货币，如果你不得不逃离这个国家，就像 1930 年代犹太人不得不离开维也纳一样，你不需要争抢黄金。你使用加密货币。如果你生活在某个倾向于没收你银行账户里所有钱的国家，那么你拥有不可没收的钱。如果你担心我们印太多美元导致恶性通胀，那么你有一个对冲 MMT 以及他们用来印钱的各种借口的工具。

turns.en: I mean, probably the scariest thing that happened in 2020 from a financial perspective is both the Republican and the Democratic party figured out that, oh, actually we can just print lots and lots of money. And the US has this reserve dollar, reserve currency status with the dollar, where most of our dollars—I think something like 70%—are held by foreigners because it's the trusted reserve currency of the world. They use it for trading oil, settling international transfers, just to hide money, to protect money from their local inflationary authorities. So because of that, when we print a dollar, 70% of that inflation effect cost is borne by the rest of the world, not borne by us. And so the US government kind of figured this out, and we printed six trillion dollars to fight the coronavirus. And so now both parties can agree that they can just print money to get out of any problem they're in. A great tweet I saw was somebody wrote that now we know what would happen if aliens were to invade the earth: the Fed would just lower interest rates. The Fed would cut interest rates, right? And so unfortunately, that's just the situation we're in. So they're going to keep doing that until at some point the rest of the world throws in the towel and says, 'This dollar thing is not working for us.' And what we rely upon is that the European euro or the Chinese renminbi and these other currencies are in worse situations. But that's not necessarily always true. I think there are well-managed currencies like the Swiss franc and the Singaporean dollar. There's also hard assets like gold and crypto and real estate, and even the run-up in tech stocks at some levels, because equities are a tax-efficient inflation hedge, or relatively tax-efficient inflation hedge. Because someone like Google can just raise prices, and they don't have to hire more people because they're a monopoly. They can lay off half their engineers and the company will work just fine. So people are realizing this, and there's a flight into hard assets to get away from inflation. And crypto is one of the few places where you can really put your money in and defend against something like the dollar losing its reserve currency status. And these are black swans. It's very hard to talk about black swans because obviously since the dollar became the US reserve currency, it hasn't lost the status, even when we went off the gold standard. But at the same time, the pound sterling used to be the reserve currency of the world, and it lost its status at one point. Money used to be gold, and that went away. So it's not inconceivable that'll happen. And if it were to happen, then you would basically have the true reckoning. Then our ability to print our way out of recessions would go away. You would probably have an inflationary collapse in the US, and you would just see our global economic system start breaking down. In those scenarios, crypto does really well, assuming the internet stays up. There are scenarios where the internet goes down too, and then all hell breaks loose.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，从金融角度来看，2020 年发生的最可怕的事情可能是共和党和民主党都发现，哦，实际上我们可以大量印钞。而美国拥有美元储备货币地位，其中大部分美元——我认为大约 70%——由外国人持有，因为它是世界信任的储备货币。他们用它来交易石油、结算国际转账、隐藏资金、保护资金免受当地通胀当局的影响。因此，当我们印一美元时，70% 的通胀影响成本由世界其他地区承担，而不是我们。所以美国政府有点明白了这一点，我们印了六万亿美元来对抗冠状病毒。现在两党都同意他们可以通过印钞来解决任何问题。我看到一条很棒的推文，有人写道，现在我们知道了如果外星人入侵地球会发生什么：美联储只会降低利率。美联储会降息，对吧？不幸的是，这就是我们目前的处境。所以他们会继续这样做，直到某个时候世界其他地区认输，说‘美元这玩意儿对我们不管用了’。我们所依赖的是欧元、人民币等其他货币的情况更糟。但这并不总是正确的。我认为有一些管理良好的货币，比如瑞士法郎和新加坡元。还有像黄金、加密货币和房地产这样的硬资产，甚至科技股在某种程度上也在上涨，因为股票是一种税收高效的通货膨胀对冲工具，或者相对税收高效的通货膨胀对冲工具。因为像 Google 这样的公司可以涨价，而且他们不需要雇佣更多人，因为他们是垄断企业。他们可以解雇一半的工程师，公司照样运转良好。所以人们意识到了这一点，资金正在涌入硬资产以逃避通胀。而加密货币是少数几个真正可以投入资金并防御美元失去储备货币地位之类情况的地方之一。这些是黑天鹅事件。谈论黑天鹅事件非常困难，因为显然自从美元成为美国储备货币以来，它从未失去过这一地位，即使我们脱离了金本位。但与此同时，英镑曾经是世界储备货币，它在某个时候失去了这一地位。货币曾经是黄金，而黄金也消失了。所以这种情况并非不可能发生。如果发生，那么基本上就会迎来真正的清算。那么我们通过印钞摆脱衰退的能力就会消失。美国可能会出现通胀崩溃，我们的全球经济体系就会开始崩溃。在这些情况下，加密货币表现良好，假设互联网保持正常。也存在互联网瘫痪的情况，那时一切都会乱套。

turns.en: You better have golden guns but we're not talking about that. Cigarettes and tampons, that's great for your water. Yeah, that's right. Well, bullets are the ultimate one, right? As we also saw in 2020, we have 5 million new gun owners in the US. But it just depends how much of a prepper you are, how paranoid you want to be, and how many standard deviations out you want to handle risk. But I think this is the year where a lot of people feel like risks that were inconceivable have suddenly gotten pulled in.
turns.zh: 你最好有金枪，但我们不是在说那个。香烟和卫生棉条，对你的水有好处。没错。子弹是终极的，对吧？正如我们在 2020 年也看到的，美国有 500 万新枪主。但这取决于你有多准备、多想偏执，以及你想处理多少个标准差的风险。但我认为今年很多人觉得那些曾经不可想象的风险突然被拉近了。

ts.sec: Newbie question on using crypto in a crisis

turns.en: Let me ask a dumb newbie question as a hypothetical. So speaking as someone who is a great example of someone who can use words associated with crypto but really has no fundamental understanding. I'm the guy pointing at the bird who thinks he's got it all figured out because he's able to name the yellow-throated warbler. I don't think I have it all figured out. But let's just say I managed somehow, like a thousand monkeys typing out Shakespeare on a million laptops given infinite time, I somehow manage to get a bunch of cryptocurrency onto a hardware ledger, or a hardware wallet rather. So I have this thing. US is going to hell in a handbasket. I somehow manage to get that to fill in the blank, I'll just arbitrarily choose Spain. Like I land in Spain, I'm like 'Oh my god, that was so close, thank god I got out in time.' I managed to make it over. How the hell do you buy an apartment or pay your rent or anything like that with crypto when it still feels like the crypto landscape and the tools are kind of computers pre-graphical user interface? Do you know what I mean? Even smart people can't quite figure it out. But what would you, how would you if that were two years in the future?
turns.zh: 让我问一个愚蠢的新手问题，作为假设。作为一个能用加密货币相关词汇但根本没有基本理解的人，我是很好的例子。我就是那个指着鸟、以为自己全懂了的人，因为能说出黄喉莺的名字。我不认为我全懂了。但假设我 somehow 像无限时间下一百万台笔记本电脑上一千只猴子打出莎士比亚那样， somehow 把一堆加密货币弄到了一个硬件账本或硬件钱包上。所以我有这个东西。美国正在走向毁灭。我 somehow 把它弄到空白处，我就随便选西班牙。比如我降落在西班牙，我想‘天哪，太险了，谢天谢地我及时逃出来了。’我成功过来了。你怎么用加密货币买公寓或付房租之类的？因为感觉加密货币领域和工具还是像图形用户界面之前的电脑。你懂我的意思吗？即使聪明人也搞不太明白。但如果那是两年后，你会怎么做？

ts.sec: Practical steps for using crypto in a crisis

turns.en: I mean, I think that's unlikely, but let's just say. For most of my friends, the scenario I recommend is like: you go buy it on something like Coinbase, CoinList, whatever. You move it into a dedicated custodian like an Anchorage or a BitGo. Then you kind of hold it there. Now if the stuff is hitting the fan and you decide you want to get out of town, then you contact your rep there, you move it into a hardware wallet, and then you flee the country, right? Or go wherever you're going. And when you land on the other side, now you have this crypto in your hardware wallet. In theory, you can log into a local crypto exchange, go through their verification processes, and upload it. Now you've got Bitcoin that can be traded for cash. Or you can go find any of the local Bitcoin meetup people and groups, the enthusiasts, the so-called maximalists, the die-hard believers, and the holders, and you can trade any of them Bitcoin for cash. Now in the distant future, it's possible that people will just take Bitcoin directly because it is a fungible, high-quality hard asset that can be transported and held like cash. But if you can't do that, you can always find either a local believer or you can find a crypto exchange where you can convert it.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，我认为那不太可能，但假设如此。对我的大多数朋友，我推荐的场景是：你在 Coinbase、CoinList 之类的地方买。然后把它转移到像 Anchorage 或 BitGo 这样的专用托管机构。然后你就把它放在那里。如果事情变得糟糕，你决定要离开城镇，那么你联系你的代表，把它转移到硬件钱包，然后逃离国家，对吧？或者去你要去的地方。当你到达另一边时，现在你的硬件钱包里有这些加密货币。理论上，你可以登录当地的加密货币交易所，通过他们的验证流程，然后上传。现在你有了可以兑换成现金的比特币。或者你可以去找任何当地的比特币聚会人群和团体，那些爱好者、所谓的 maximalists、死忠信徒和持有者，你可以和他们中的任何人用比特币兑换现金。在遥远的未来，人们可能会直接接受比特币，因为它是一种可互换、高质量的硬资产，可以像现金一样运输和持有。但如果你做不到，你总能找到当地的信徒或加密货币交易所来兑换。

ts.sec: Why Bitcoin's believers make it valuable

turns.en: The thing that makes Bitcoin so interesting is not that it's necessarily the best technology. It is definitely robust and it's the OG. What makes it really interesting is it has the most die-hard believers. And because it has the most die-hard believers, as long as there's five thousand or fifty thousand wealthy, brilliant people who—just that the wealthy part is really important—they will always trade it for you, trade it with you for hard assets. You know, I think recently MicroStrategy started, it's a company, it started moving half of its treasury into Bitcoin. And the CEO Michael Saylor was quoted as saying something along the lines of 'I can't believe people are willing to sell me Bitcoin.' Right? He figured out what it was and then he was just like 'I want to get as much as possible.' So you know, here, take pieces of my company but give me Bitcoin. And there are lots of people like him. There are lots and lots of people like him, and they exist in every major country in the world. And as long as there are believers—because it's as much a movement or religion as it is a currency—and because what is a currency at the end of the day? Money is just the bubble that never pops, right? It's just if we all agree tomorrow that if we all manage to come upon the story tomorrow that it's not the US dollar, it's actually the Euro that is really money, then the world will switch to the Euro. Heck, if we agreed that clamshells were money, the world would switch to clamshells. Now obviously there are underlying characteristics that make Euros and dollars better than clamshells, and so therefore we converge upon those. But if the world were to decide that Bitcoin is better money than US dollars, the world would switch to Bitcoin. It's a story, it's a consensus belief. And the beauty of Bitcoin is that there are these die-hard maximalists. And I fight with them on the internet all the time. They don't like me, by the way, so I've had Twitter battles with them running. But I still really appreciate that they exist because they are actually the core of what makes Bitcoin always tradeable, always valuable. There's always some guy in some location somewhere in the world who will give you his house for Bitcoin. And as long as that is true—and I don't see why it will stop being true because if anything, more and more people are being added to that list every day—it has real value and has redeemable value.
turns.zh: 比特币之所以如此有趣，并不一定是因为它是最好的技术。它确实很稳健，而且是 OG。真正有趣的是它拥有最死忠的信徒。正因为有最死忠的信徒，只要有五千或五万富有、聪明的人——富有这部分非常重要——他们总会为你交易，用硬资产和你交易。你知道，最近 MicroStrategy 这家公司开始将其一半的国库转移到比特币。CEO Michael Saylor 被引述说‘我不敢相信有人愿意把比特币卖给我。’对吧？他搞清楚了它是什么，然后他就想‘我要尽可能多地得到它。’所以，给你，拿我的公司股份，但给我比特币。有很多像他这样的人。有很多很多像他这样的人，他们存在于世界上每个主要国家。只要有信徒——因为它既是一场运动或宗教，也是一种货币——因为货币归根结底是什么？钱只是一个永远不会破灭的泡沫，对吧？如果我们明天都同意，如果我们明天都达成共识，说不是美元，而是欧元才是真正的钱，那么世界就会转向欧元。见鬼，如果我们同意蛤壳是钱，世界就会转向蛤壳。当然，有一些底层特征使欧元和美元优于蛤壳，所以我们趋同于它们。但如果世界决定比特币比美元更好，世界就会转向比特币。这是一个故事，一个共识信仰。比特币的美妙之处在于有这些死忠的 maximalists。我一直在网上和他们争论。顺便说一句，他们不喜欢我，所以我一直在和他们进行 Twitter 大战。但我仍然非常感激他们的存在，因为他们实际上是比特币始终可交易、始终有价值的核心。世界上某个地方总有一个人会用他的房子换比特币。只要这是真的——我看不出它会停止成为真的，因为实际上每天都有越来越多的人加入这个名单——它就有真正的价值，有可赎回的价值。

turns.en: What do you think the conceivable near term looks like with the possible entrance of more institutional investors? In other words, if we see—let me ask a better question. It was very noteworthy and newsworthy when Paul Tudor Jones wrote his memo detailing why he was investing, I think it was something like 100 million into Bitcoin or Bitcoin-like equivalence, some type of crypto equivalent, if not crypto itself. Do you anticipate that more institutionals are going to come in, sovereign wealth funds, things like that? And how do you think the story and the landscape could change over the next handful of years?
turns.zh: 你认为随着更多机构投资者可能进入，可预见的近期会是什么样子？换句话说，如果我们看到——让我问一个更好的问题。当 Paul Tudor Jones 写备忘录详细说明他为什么投资时，这非常引人注目，我想是大约 1 亿美元投入比特币或类似比特币的等价物，某种加密货币等价物，如果不是加密货币本身的话。你预计会有更多机构投资者、主权财富基金等进入吗？你认为未来几年故事和格局会如何变化？

ts.sec: Inevitable institutional adoption and remaining risks

turns.en: I think it's inevitable. Look, Bitcoin and crypto still have risks, right? A lot of these algorithms and schemes are new. Even Bitcoin through this proof-of-work mechanism, you could argue that it tends towards centralization because it gets managed by these anonymous miners and data centers, and data centers of economies of scale, and so it will end up being more centralized than people would like. There are up-and-coming competitors like Ethereum. There's privacy issues—you know, on the Bitcoin blockchain, you can be tracked forever, so it's not perfect. You know, people talk about quantum computing breaking it. I don't think that's true; I think you can upgrade the encryption algorithm just as well or better. But there are always potential problems, right, which are not completely resolved. But every time we face one of these reckonings and that problem gets resolved, the value goes up, the story becomes stronger. And so the same way when Paul Tudor Jones or...
turns.zh: 我认为这是不可避免的。看，比特币和加密货币仍然有风险，对吧？很多这些算法和方案都是新的。即使是比特币通过这种工作量证明机制，你可以说它倾向于中心化，因为它由这些匿名矿工和数据中心管理，而数据中心有规模经济，所以它最终会比人们希望的更中心化。有像以太坊这样的后起之秀。还有隐私问题——你知道，在比特币区块链上，你可以被永久追踪，所以它并不完美。你知道，人们谈论量子计算会破解它。我不认为这是真的；我认为你可以升级加密算法，同样好或更好。但总是有潜在的问题，对吧，这些问题没有完全解决。但每次我们面对这些清算，问题得到解决时，价值就会上升，故事就会变得更强大。所以同样，当 Paul Tudor Jones 或……

turns.en: MicroStrategy by Bitcoin, the story is becoming stronger. The set of believers is increasing, the validation is increasing, and now more people can come in and hang their hat on this and say, 'Okay, if Paul Tudor Jones agrees and Michael Saylor agrees, then I agree to be in that set of people who believe that this is going to be the new way to store value and wealth.' So now I'm joining their wealth storage scheme, and the early people then get rewarded for it.
turns.zh: MicroStrategy 通过比特币，这个故事越来越强。信徒群体在扩大，验证在增加，现在更多人能加入并以此为据，说：‘好吧，如果保罗·都铎·琼斯同意，迈克尔·塞勒也同意，那我也同意成为相信这将是新的价值储存和财富方式的人群中的一员。’所以我现在加入他们的财富存储计划，早期的人因此得到回报。

turns.en: So one way to think about Bitcoin in particular, or even actually any of the cryptocurrencies, is it's a Swiss bank account, but it's a Swiss bank account with finite space. And if you want shelf space in that Swiss bank account with finite space, you have to buy out one of the existing holders in that space. So imagine a Swiss bank account that's impregnable, it's a new one, no government can break into it, it's secured by consensus across millions of people using massive computation power on the globe. And if you want one of those safety deposit boxes, you got to buy it from someone who's already there. So as long as the demand for new people trying to get into the Swiss bank account is greater than the supply of people who are trying to get out of that Swiss bank account, then you're going to pay more for that box, and that's where the speculative power of it comes from.
turns.zh: 所以思考比特币，甚至任何加密货币的一种方式，就是它是一个瑞士银行账户，但这是一个空间有限的瑞士银行账户。如果你想在那个空间有限的瑞士银行账户中获得货架空间，你必须从现有的持有者手中买下那个空间。想象一个坚不可摧的瑞士银行账户，一个全新的，没有政府能闯入，它由全球数百万人使用巨大算力达成的共识来保障安全。如果你想要其中一个保险箱，你必须从已经在那里的人那里购买。所以只要想进入这个瑞士银行账户的新人需求大于想离开这个账户的供应，你就要为那个箱子支付更多，这就是其投机力量的来源。

turns.en: And I think it's going to take a good crisis to really see what crypto is worth. And actually so far it's been weird because people have said that crypto failed the crisis test with COVID, right? I mean, it didn't skyrocket as everybody fled the US dollar. Bitcoin didn't go to $100k. But that kind of shows you how resilient the dollar is and how far we are from losing global reserve currency status. But at the same time, it didn't collapse either. It went down briefly as people needed cash to meet various margin calls and loans, but overall stayed very stable. And I would say the number of people who have gotten involved in crypto recently as a true wealth protection mechanism is the largest I've ever seen. So I do think that the holder base has gone up.
turns.zh: 我认为需要一场真正的危机才能看清加密货币的价值。实际上到目前为止很奇怪，因为人们说加密货币在新冠疫情中未能通过危机测试，对吧？我的意思是，它没有像所有人逃离美元那样飙升。比特币没有涨到 10 万美元。但这恰恰显示了美元的韧性，以及我们距离失去全球储备货币地位还有多远。但与此同时，它也没有崩溃。它短暂下跌，因为人们需要现金来满足各种追加保证金和贷款，但总体上保持非常稳定。我要说，最近参与加密货币作为真正财富保护机制的人数是我见过最多的。所以我确实认为持有者基础已经上升。

turns.en: I don't know if we're going to see like real $3,000 bitcoin ever again in my lifetime. We might see zero if something breaks completely, but I don't think we're going to see a bunch of people leaving and losing faith in it, and that's why it goes to $3k. Bitcoin tends to hit these highs, then crash back down to a plateau, hang out in a plateau for a while, and then go back to a new high. And I kind of feel like we're around the $10k level, who knows? I don't want to make price predictions, but I feel like there's a stronger base of holders now than there's ever been.
turns.zh: 我不知道在我有生之年是否还会看到真正的 3000 美元比特币。如果某些东西完全崩溃，我们可能会看到零，但我不认为会看到很多人离开并失去信心，从而导致它跌到 3000 美元。比特币往往会达到这些高点，然后暴跌回一个平台，在平台上停留一段时间，然后再回到新高。我感觉我们大概在 1 万美元水平，谁知道呢？我不想做价格预测，但我觉得现在的持有者基础比以往任何时候都更强大。

turns.en: So each one of these people comes and validates it. Paul Tudor Jones validates it for other hedge fund managers, hedge fund managers validate it for sovereign wealth funds, sovereign wealth funds validate it for central banks. Eventually some country out there is going to say, 'Actually, we inflated our currency too much, our currency collapsed, nobody trusts us anymore, we have to adopt a new national currency. Instead of pegging to the dollar, let's peg to Bitcoin, or let's use Bitcoin, or let's use some other cryptocurrency, or let's be clever and buy up a bunch of Bitcoin and then announce we're going to use Bitcoin, and then Bitcoin will skyrocket and we're all rich.' So I think there are other scenarios where it ends up being a lot more valuable, but it's going to be stumbling steps forward and backward. It's not going to be in one fell swoop until it is, right? That's the nature of black swans: you can't predict them, when it happens it happens suddenly.
turns.zh: 所以这些人一个个都来验证它。保罗·都铎·琼斯为其他对冲基金经理验证，对冲基金经理为主权财富基金验证，主权财富基金为中央银行验证。最终某个国家会说：‘实际上，我们货币通胀太严重，货币崩溃了，没人再信任我们，我们必须采用一种新的国家货币。与其盯住美元，不如盯住比特币，或者使用比特币，或者使用其他加密货币，或者聪明点，买进一堆比特币，然后宣布我们要使用比特币，这样比特币就会飙升，我们都发财了。’所以我认为还有其他场景，最终它会变得更有价值，但过程会是跌跌撞撞，前进后退。不会一蹴而就，直到它发生，对吧？这就是黑天鹅的本质：你无法预测，发生时突然发生。

turns.en: I had a friend who used to tell me—he's now a hardcore Bitcoiner, he actually helped write a book on Bitcoin now—but when I first talked about Bitcoin years ago, he said, 'What's the big deal? If I ever have to flee the country, I'll just buy Bitcoin then.' And I said, 'Yeah, when you have to flee the country, your entire fortune's going to buy you one Bitcoin, right?' That's the problem: it's supply and demand. So you can't wait till the last second. And I do think more and more smart people are coming into it every single day.
turns.zh: 我有一个朋友曾经告诉我——他现在是核心比特币信徒，实际上还帮忙写了一本关于比特币的书——但多年前我第一次谈论比特币时，他说：‘有什么大不了的？如果我不得不逃离这个国家，我到时候买比特币就行了。’我说：‘是啊，当你不得不逃离时，你全部财产只能买一个比特币，对吧？’问题就在这里：这是供需关系。所以你不能等到最后一刻。我确实认为每天都有越来越多的聪明人加入进来。

turns.en: And I think the psychological dynamics of crypto volatility are a lot like a casino slot machine: variable reward with high variability, where you just see things spiking and dropping, and then they stay flat, and then they spike and they drop, in a way that is unlike most equities that you or I would buy on the public markets. Even Wall Street at some level is a big casino with a potential positive expected value, creating some value for society in terms of hedging and liquidity for companies raising money, and so on. And crypto's doing the same thing: it's a global 24/7/365 casino where anybody in the world can play. But through decentralized finance underneath, you're actually making loans, protecting wealth, storing it, letting people buy derivatives, letting people hedge. So all of these things are coming up: we now have insurance in the crypto markets, we now have lending in the crypto markets, we have shorting in the crypto markets, we have computation going on that requires crypto underneath.
turns.zh: 我认为加密货币波动的心理动态很像赌场的老虎机：高变异性的可变奖励，你会看到价格飙升和下跌，然后持平，然后再次飙升和下跌，这种方式不同于你我会在公开市场上购买的大多数股票。甚至华尔街在某种程度上也是一个大型赌场，具有潜在的正期望值，为社会创造一些价值，比如对冲和为公司融资提供流动性等等。加密货币也在做同样的事情：它是一个全球 7 天 24 小时 365 天的赌场，世界上任何人都可以参与。但通过底层的去中心化金融，你实际上在发放贷款、保护财富、存储财富、让人们购买衍生品、让人们进行对冲。所以所有这些都在涌现：我们现在有加密货币市场的保险，有借贷，有做空，有需要底层加密货币的计算。

turns.en: Look, if two computers are talking to each other, if two mini AIs are talking to each other at high speed, exchanging resources to run a company, how are they going to exchange money? You think they're going to send US dollars through PayPal or through Fedwire? Hell no, they're going to use crypto. Crypto is the native currency of the internet. Of course the internet is going to have its own currency. Otherwise it's like saying the internet's going to use email through the USPS. It's going to have its own native on-chain, on-wire protocol for communicating data.
turns.zh: 看，如果两台计算机在互相通信，如果两个迷你 AI 在高速通信，交换资源来运营一家公司，它们将如何交换货币？你认为它们会通过 PayPal 或 Fedwire 发送美元吗？绝对不会，它们会使用加密货币。加密货币是互联网的原生货币。互联网当然会有自己的货币。否则就像说互联网将通过美国邮政服务使用电子邮件一样。它将有自己的原生链上、在线协议来传输数据。

turns.en: And so what cryptocurrencies really are: Bitcoin isn't a thing, Bitcoin is not like there's a coin sitting somewhere. Bitcoin is an entry in a virtual ledger, and that virtual ledger is maintained by tons of machines running across the world. And what you're doing is you're communicating value securely across the internet with no third parties in the middle validating that that is the correct communication. It's done by the entire network at once. And you're communicating and transmitting scarcity and value to the internet, and that's a new thing. What the internet gave us before was digital abundance: I can make copies of everything. And that was a very big idea: I can make one podcast and ship it to everybody, I can make one web page and ship it to everybody. That was a very big idea and created huge fortunes and huge revolutions. The same way digital scarcity is a very big idea.
turns.zh: 所以加密货币真正是什么：比特币不是一个东西，不是像有一枚硬币放在某处。比特币是虚拟账本中的一个条目，而这个虚拟账本由全球运行的大量机器维护。你所做的是在互联网上安全地传输价值，中间没有第三方验证这是正确的通信。它由整个网络一次性完成。你在向互联网传输稀缺性和价值，这是一件新事物。互联网之前给我们的是数字丰裕：我可以复制一切。这是一个非常伟大的想法：我可以制作一个播客并发送给所有人，我可以制作一个网页并发送给所有人。这创造了巨大的财富和巨大的革命。同样，数字稀缺性也是一个非常伟大的想法。

turns.en: Scarcity is an equally interesting idea. It means I can only have one of this thing. If Naval has a bitcoin, then Tim Ferriss doesn't have that bitcoin, or vice versa. That ability to create scarcity and transmit scarcity and value through the internet is just as important as the ability to create abundance and transmit that through the internet. So the native language of the internet in communications and protocols around valuable things and on finance is going to be in cryptocurrencies. It's not going to be any other way.
turns.zh: 稀缺性同样是一个有趣的概念。它意味着我只能拥有这个东西的一份。如果 Naval 有一个比特币，那么 Tim Ferriss 就没有那个比特币，反之亦然。通过互联网创造稀缺性并传递稀缺性和价值的能力，与创造丰裕并通过互联网传递丰裕的能力同样重要。因此，互联网在关于有价值事物的通信和协议以及金融方面的原生语言将是加密货币。没有其他方式。

turns.en: I want to bring up two more tweets and then we can bring this to a close and put a bow on it. First, I just want to mention again—and I very rarely do this—but I am very happy with how this discussion turned out. If people want more of the history and the design of cryptocurrency, which is endlessly fascinating, just Google Nick Szabo, S-Z-A-B-O. And then if you're interested in diving into audio, Naval and I spoke with them.
turns.zh: 我想再提两条推文，然后我们就可以收尾了。首先，我想再次提到——我很少这样做——但我对这次讨论的结果非常满意。如果有人想了解更多关于加密货币的历史和设计，这非常迷人，只需谷歌 Nick Szabo，S-Z-A-B-O。如果你对音频感兴趣，Naval 和我与他们聊过。

turns.en: Two tweets, both from September. One I'm going to read just because I agree with it super strongly, and I also have observed you following this advice very well. Which is: all self-help boils down to choose long-term over short-term. I would modify the tweet to say though: all truly effective self-help boils down to choose long-term over short-term. And I mean that is the entire challenge. Long-term thinking gets you long-term results. I've said this a thousand different ways: in business, you want to play long-term games with long-term people. In modern life, addictions and dealing with the avalanche of abundance of things thrown at you—video games are a trap, a shadow career that substitutes for your real career. Smoking weed or drinking alcohol substitutes for pleasure through simpler things like sunlight and walks. Porn substitutes for sex. This goes on and on.
turns.zh: 两条推文，都是九月份的。我要读一条，因为我非常同意它，而且我也观察到你在很好地遵循这个建议。那就是：所有自助归根结底就是选择长期而非短期。不过我会修改这条推文：所有真正有效的自助归根结底就是选择长期而非短期。我的意思是，这就是全部的挑战。长期思维带来长期结果。我已经用一千种不同的方式说过：在商业中，你想与长期的人玩长期游戏。在现代生活中，成瘾和应对大量抛给你的丰裕事物——电子游戏是一个陷阱，一个替代你真正职业的影子职业。吸大麻或喝酒替代了通过更简单的事物如阳光和散步获得的快乐。色情替代了性。这可以一直列下去。

turns.en: I thought you were saying walking, sunlight, and porn. I was like, yeah, that sounds about the trifecta.
turns.zh: 我以为你在说散步、阳光和色情。我当时想，是啊，这听起来像是三件套。

turns.en: But look at the related tweet I had: the modern devil is cheap dopamine. And some interesting people on Twitter pointed out to me actually the ancient devil is cheap dopamine as well. But in modern times, we've just hyper-accelerated it. The modern mind is overstimulated, the modern body is understimulated. We've taken all the creature comforts and maximized them a thousand times. So literally all success in life boils down to a variation of the marshmallow test: can the kid hold off eating the marshmallow for 15 minutes? Then they get two marshmallows. The claim is that turned out to be a big predictor for future success, although like many psych experiments, I don't think it replicates well. But anyway, it just boils down to long-term versus short-term. If you can just adopt long-term thinking in your mindset, you're going to have a much easier life. Or as our favorite trainer, Jocko Willink, says: hard choices, easy life; easy choices, hard life. That's it. So can you take a long-term view on anything? Compound interest applies everywhere: in relationships, in money, in health and fitness. Can you change your habits to read the kinds of books that will serve you best in the long term, the foundational books? Can you change your eating habits so that you're eating healthy food as opposed to unhealthy food? Can you create an exercise routine that you can do every day or every other day so that it's a consistent part of your life? I think a lot of it boils down to just choosing the long term or the short term. And all the good hacks basically help make the long-term choice palatable or help inspire you to keep your eyes focused on the long term.
turns.zh: 但看看我发的相关推文：现代的魔鬼是廉价的多巴胺。推特上一些有趣的人指出，实际上古代的魔鬼也是廉价的多巴胺。但在现代，我们只是把它超加速了。现代的大脑过度刺激，现代的身体刺激不足。我们把所有的舒适享受都放大了一千倍。所以实际上，生活中所有的成功都归结为棉花糖测试的一个变种：孩子能否忍住 15 分钟不吃棉花糖？然后他们得到两个棉花糖。据说这能很好地预测未来的成功，尽管像许多心理实验一样，我不认为它能很好地复制。但无论如何，这归结为长期与短期。如果你能在心态上采用长期思维，你的生活就会轻松得多。或者像我们最喜欢的教练 Jocko Willink 说的：艰难的选择，轻松的生活；轻松的选择，艰难的生活。就是这样。所以，你能对任何事情采取长期视角吗？复利无处不在：在关系中，在金钱上，在健康和健身中。你能改变你的习惯，去读那些长期对你最有帮助的书，那些基础书籍吗？你能改变你的饮食习惯，吃健康食品而不是不健康食品吗？你能建立一个每天或每隔一天都能做的锻炼计划，让它成为你生活中持续的一部分吗？我认为很多归结为只是选择长期或短期。所有好的技巧基本上都是帮助让长期选择变得可口，或者帮助激励你保持专注于长期。

turns.en: An example is if you're doing something that you love, if you're running a business that you enjoy, it's probably going to be more fun than playing a video game. If you come home at night from work and your first instinct is to play a video game, you're suffering at work. You're working just as hard in the video game, but the rewards are all fake because they're going to change the rules on you, or some 13-year-old kid on the internet will destroy you because they play that video game all day long. I used to be a hardcore video gamer; I had a lot of fun doing it, and I did learn playing games for sure. But at this age, if I pick up a video game, I'm doing it to get away from suffering, and I'm not really getting anything out of it. I could play the video game of investing, or I could play the video game of starting companies, or I could play the video game of going and playing tennis, and that would be good for my health. So I think a lot of it is about finding the thing that you can do that is fun for you, that looks like play, feels like play to you, but looks like work to others, that you can do that sustains you for the long term.
turns.zh: 一个例子是，如果你在做你热爱的事情，如果你在经营一家你喜欢的公司，那可能比玩电子游戏更有趣。如果你晚上下班回家，第一反应是玩电子游戏，那说明你在工作中很痛苦。你在电子游戏中同样努力，但奖励都是假的，因为规则会变，或者网上某个 13 岁的孩子会打败你，因为他们整天都在玩那个游戏。我曾经是一个铁杆游戏玩家；我玩得很开心，也确实从游戏中有所学习。但到了这个年纪，如果我拿起一个电子游戏，我是为了逃避痛苦，而且我并没有真正从中得到什么。我可以玩投资这个游戏，或者我可以玩创业这个游戏，或者我可以玩打网球这个游戏，这对我的健康有好处。所以我认为很多是关于找到你能做的事情，它对你来说很有趣，看起来像玩，感觉像玩，但对别人来说像工作，你能长期做下去并维持自己。

turns.en: For example, in foods, what you want to find is the health food that other people consider healthy and has a nutritionally healthy profile, but that I find tasty. Because if I can create those foods, if I can become a good cook and create great foods that I enjoy eating, and I can eat them regularly but they're actually super healthy, that's amazing. That's a hack. Now I'm eating for the long term, but I'm not giving up too much of the short-term pleasure. The same way, being in a good relationship is going to be better than watching porn. Having a good career or working an enjoyable job, or hacking on some code that I enjoy, is going to be far more productive than playing a video game. I don't want to sound preachy, but I will just say that as for my love for reading, it came from another one of my tweets: read what you love until you love to read. So just fall in love with the idea of reading itself. It's okay to read the junk food stuff; just fall in love with reading. Eventually, you'll get bored of the simple stuff and you'll go to the more interesting stuff. I love to read enough that I don't like watching TV, I don't watch movies. People start talking about shows they're watching on Netflix, and I give them this empty look. I don't watch shows on Netflix; they're not interesting. It's far more interesting to me to go for a walk and be meditative, and I'll be in a very happy place, or to read a book which will be intellectually stimulating. Sure, once in a while, watch a movie with friends or with my wife, but it's a social thing. I'm doing it as a way of being on the same common ground as them. I would never watch a movie on my own unless maybe I was trapped in a 15-hour airplane flight, I was really bored, and I ran out of everything else to do.
turns.zh: 例如，在食物方面，你想找到的是别人认为健康、营养健康但你觉得美味的健康食品。因为如果我能做出这些食物，如果我能成为一个好厨师，做出我喜欢吃的美味食物，并且我能经常吃，但它们实际上非常健康，那就太棒了。那是一个技巧。现在我在为长期而吃，但我没有放弃太多的短期快乐。同样，一段好的关系会比看色情片更好。拥有一个好的职业或一份愉快的工作，或者编写一些我喜欢的代码，会比玩电子游戏高效得多。我不想显得说教，但我要说，我对阅读的热爱来自于我的另一条推文：读你喜欢的，直到你喜欢阅读。所以，只要爱上阅读本身。读垃圾食品类的东西也没关系；只要爱上阅读。最终，你会对简单的东西感到厌倦，然后转向更有趣的东西。我非常喜欢阅读，以至于我不喜欢看电视，我不看电影。人们开始谈论他们在 Netflix 上看的节目，我给他们一个茫然的表情。我不看 Netflix 上的节目；它们不有趣。对我来说，散步和冥想有趣得多，我会处于一个非常快乐的状态，或者读一本能激发智力的书。当然，偶尔和朋友或妻子一起看电影，但那是社交活动。我这样做是为了和他们有共同话题。我永远不会一个人看电影，除非我被困在 15 小时的航班上，真的很无聊，而且所有其他事情都做完了。

turns.en: Find people that doesn't take work to be around. The best relationships don't feel like work. You make the other person happy by being yourself; they make you happy by being themselves. Everyone's honest, no one's putting on an act they can't carry on for the next decade. Same thing with friendships. The best friendships are formed out of nothing—not because you went to school, not because you studied the same things, not because you're working together, not because you enjoy rugby or whatever. It's because your chemistry matches, your temperament is similar, and so you can be friends for 30 or 50 years. The compound interest in relationships part ironically means that the best relationships—whether friendships, family, or love—are the ones where you don't have to work too hard at them. So you don't have to sustain that workload for the rest of your life, and you can do it effortlessly.
turns.zh: 找到那些相处起来不费劲的人。最好的关系不会让人觉得是负担。你做自己就能让对方开心，他们做自己也能让你开心。每个人都坦诚，没有人在演戏，而且能持续十年。友谊也是如此。最好的友谊是自然形成的——不是因为一起上学，不是因为学同样的东西，不是因为一起工作，也不是因为都喜欢橄榄球什么的。而是因为你们气场相合，性情相似，所以可以做 30 年、50 年的朋友。关系中的复利效应讽刺地意味着，最好的关系——无论是友谊、亲情还是爱情——都是那些不需要你太费力的。这样你就不必一辈子维持那种努力，可以毫不费力地经营。

turns.en: Yeah, totally. One of your quotes that I think of often—and I might be paraphrasing this—is: 'If you want to avoid conflict, rule number one is avoid people who are constantly involved with conflict.' Something along those lines.
turns.zh: 完全同意。我经常想起你的一句话——可能是我转述的——'如果你想避免冲突，第一条规则就是避开那些总是卷入冲突的人。'大概是这个意思。

turns.en: For example, when you're in a relationship, just watch how the other person treats their worst enemy, because eventually they'll just redefine you as an enemy, and you'll get to feel that behavior. So I think the number one criteria I look for in a relationship is that a person has to be kind. They just have to be a nice person. Because eventually, in a certain context, you can always be reclassified from friend to enemy. So you just want to see the boundaries of someone's ethics. If you see someone being bad to a server, or engaging in unethical behavior, or suing other people, or fighting other people all the time, it's only a matter of time before they fight you. So just stay away from these high-conflict people. Everyone has conflict; no one is clean. But that said, there are definitely people who engage in conflict regularly and make it a part of their lifestyle. Just walk away. It's not worth it. There are plenty of people who are low-conflict and easy to get along with.
turns.zh: 比如，在一段关系中，观察对方如何对待他们最坏的敌人，因为最终他们可能会重新把你定义为敌人，你也会感受到那种行为。所以我在关系中寻找的首要标准是，一个人必须善良。他们必须是个好人。因为在某些情况下，你总是可能从朋友被重新归类为敌人。所以你要看清一个人道德的边界。如果你看到有人对服务员态度恶劣，或者有不道德的行为，或者总是起诉别人、跟人吵架，那他们跟你吵架只是时间问题。所以远离这些高冲突的人。每个人都有冲突，没有人是干净的。但话说回来，确实有些人经常卷入冲突，并把它变成生活方式的一部分。走开就好，不值得。有很多低冲突、容易相处的人。

turns.en: Low conflict, low maintenance, who can also be brutally candid when necessary, right? Which doesn't equal high conflict.
turns.zh: 低冲突、低维护，同时必要时也能直言不讳，对吧？这并不等于高冲突。

turns.en: Well, the one thing I've noticed—and I haven't written the tweet on this because I've had a hard time figuring how to say it—but the people I want to spend time around these days, when I look at what the common characteristic is, they're highly self-aware. They're very, very aware of themselves. They're not running on autopilot. They don't get angry easily. They don't get unhappy easily. They don't take themselves too seriously. They have a certain separation from their own thoughts and personality that prevents circumstances and their personality from overwhelming them. They don't have a victim mentality. They're not caught up in some story of what happened to them when they were younger. They've had those issues for sure, but they've just gotten past them and been like, 'I don't want to be that person. I don't want to be trapped in that mindset.' They're not trying to signal all the time how important they are, or who they know, or what they've done, or how virtuous they are. They're not virtue signaling or grandstanding. They have low egos generally. I can literally plot on a line: the more self-aware somebody is, I guarantee you, the more attractive they are to a large number of people. And to the degree that I've achieved any modicum of fame—and fame is a trap, and I'm going to pay for this, I know I will pay for this—but any modicum of fame that I've achieved, I think is because I am one of the few people who has been successful in business that thinks out loud in public. And because I'm willing to think out loud in public, that's a risk that I take. It improves my thinking, but other people say, 'Oh yeah, he's somewhat self-aware, he's thinking about himself.' And I think that helps inspire other people to also say, 'Okay, it's okay for me to think about myself.' I just find hanging around self-aware people is a lot easier than people who are running on autopilot, almost like NPCs.
turns.zh: 我注意到一件事——我还没为此发过推文，因为很难表达——但如今我想花时间相处的人，他们的共同特点是高度自我觉察。他们非常了解自己。他们不是自动驾驶模式。他们不容易生气，不容易不开心，不把自己太当回事。他们与自己的想法和人格保持一定距离，这防止了环境和性格压垮他们。他们没有受害者心态。他们不会陷入小时候发生了什么事的叙事中。他们当然有过那些问题，但他们已经走出来了，并且说'我不想成为那样的人，我不想被困在那样的心态里。'他们不会一直炫耀自己有多重要、认识谁、做过什么、或者有多高尚。他们不进行道德炫耀或自我标榜。他们通常自我意识较低。我可以画一条线：一个人越有自我觉察，我保证，他们对大多数人的吸引力就越大。至于我获得的那一点点名气——名气是个陷阱，我会为此付出代价，我知道我会——但我认为我获得的那一点点名气，是因为我是少数在商业上成功却愿意公开思考的人。因为我愿意公开思考，这是我冒的风险。这改善了我的思考，但别人会说'哦，他有点自我觉察，他在思考自己。'我认为这能激励其他人也说'好吧，我也可以思考自己。'我发现和高度自我觉察的人相处，比和那些像 NPC 一样自动驾驶的人容易得多。

turns.en: NPCs? What does that mean?
turns.zh: NPC？那是什么意思？

turns.en: Non-player characters. It's a reference to certain kinds of video games where it's like a computer-controlled character. They're predictable.
turns.zh: 非玩家角色。这是某些电子游戏里的说法，指电脑控制的角色。他们是可以预测的。

ts.sec: The Game of Life and Winning to Be Free

turns.en: All right, so speaking of games, this is the final tweet, the final podcast. Here it is: 'The reason to win the game is so that you can be free of it.' And my questions about this are—wait, one more time: 'The reason to win the game is so that you can be free of it.' I would be curious to know what game you are free of, or what game you are playing so that you can be free of it.
turns.zh: 好了，说到游戏，这是最后一条推文，最后一期播客。这句话是：'赢得游戏的理由是让你能够摆脱它。'我的问题是——等一下，再说一遍：'赢得游戏的理由是让你能够摆脱它。'我很好奇你摆脱了什么游戏，或者你在玩什么游戏以便摆脱它。

turns.en: I like to think I'm free of almost all the games at this point. What are the games? All of life is games, right? You start out with the family game, you do the school game, the grades game, the getting girlfriends and boyfriends game, the getting married game, the having kids game, the making money game, the having a career game, the getting famous game, the dressing well game. You know, so much of it is social games. There are a few that are not games, like meditation—you're not doing it if you're scoring points in meditation, you're doing it wrong. Yoga, these are single-player games. So it's really the multiplayer games that suck you into society and into anxiety. Now, you kind of have to play them. You have two choices towards peace in your life. One is you can play these games and you can win them, and when you win them—this is the hard part—realizing you've won and stopped playing new ones, or setting new goals for yourself that are even higher and higher. It's like my friends who have made so much money and they continue, all they try to do is make money, and they just don't seem very happy about it. It's fine if they're happy—if they're like Elon Musk, seems like he's having a grand old time, that's fine. But if it's making you miserable and you continue doing it, and you only have one life and you kind of can't see past that, then I would argue you're playing the game too far. So there's kind of two ways out of the trap. One is not wanting something is as good as having it. I think it was Lyad Shabo who said this on Twitter, I thought it was really good. Or there's a recent Socrates quote that I retweeted, where he was taken to a market back in ancient Greece and it was full of luxurious and fine items, and he said, 'There are so many things that I do not want.'
turns.zh: 我觉得自己现在已经摆脱了几乎所有游戏。游戏是什么？生活就是游戏，对吧？你从家庭游戏开始，然后上学游戏、成绩游戏、找男女朋友游戏、结婚游戏、生孩子游戏、赚钱游戏、职业游戏、出名游戏、穿着打扮游戏。很多都是社交游戏。有些不是游戏，比如冥想——如果你在冥想中计分，那你就做错了。瑜伽，这些都是单人游戏。真正让你陷入社会和焦虑的是多人游戏。你多少得玩它们。要获得内心的平静，你有两个选择。一是你可以玩这些游戏并赢得它们，而赢得之后——最难的部分——意识到你已经赢了，不再玩新的，或者不再给自己设定越来越高的目标。就像我那些赚了很多钱的朋友，他们继续只想着赚钱，但看起来并不快乐。如果他们快乐——比如埃隆·马斯克，他看起来玩得很开心，那没问题。但如果这让你痛苦而你还在继续，而且你只有一次生命，却看不到这一点，那我认为你玩得太过了。所以有两种方式可以摆脱陷阱。一是'不想要某样东西和拥有它一样好。'我记得是 Lyad Shabo 在推特上说的，我觉得很好。或者我最近转发了一句苏格拉底的话，故事是他被带到古希腊的一个市场，那里摆满了奢侈品和精美物品，他说：'有太多东西是我并不想要的。'

turns.en: Finders and said so many things here that I do not want, and I thought that is freedom, that is power, that is self-contained, that is a person who has found himself and needs nothing outside of himself. That is so inspirational. So the same way I look upon the world and I say, well, I could just say that right? You can't just say that. If I just said there's so many things that I don't want, it's not true. I want the money, I want the girl, you know, I want the fame, or I wouldn't have gone on podcasts and I wouldn't be on Twitter. At some level that is true. So obviously there's certain things that I want. I'm not a monk, I'm not living in an ashram. But what I can do is I've gotten the things that I want and I'm careful not to want more. I don't want more fame, I don't want more money. Or if I get it, it's fine, it's part of the craft, by the bonus, you know. So I have to be careful about not unconsciously taking on new desires. If I'm taking a new desire, I better go fulfill that. So these are all just games that I'm playing, and you know, you got to play some game. You're on this planet, you're alive, you might as well play something. Now the question is what game do you play and how do you get out of the game so you're not just trapped playing that game forever. And one way is you choose your games very carefully. If you're a monk, you only choose very, very few games to play, or you play no games and you live content, blissful, harmonious, peaceful. Or the other is you play the game and you win it, and then you say I am now free of this. So what that tweet is, that tweet is the reason to play the game. So the reason to win the game is to be free of it. It's a reminder to myself that for the games that I've won, it's time to let go of them and to be free of them, and not to unconsciously double down by comparing myself to the Joneses and continuing to level up and level up and level up and level up, and then one day you die and then you're like, okay, that was pointless. Like, you know, I did all that work for what? For nothing. So if you're not enjoying it anymore, if you've already won the game by the definition you had when you started playing the game, and one hack is to set the definition early on so that when you go past it you know you won, to not get trapped into playing this game forever and just living in some anxious future as opposed to actually enjoying yourself in the present. You have to know when to stop playing the game. And so to me, the reason to play these games is to win them and then you can be free of them, and to see what else then that you want to do or not do, not to keep playing the same game forever. Because these games, the adult games, are very cleverly designed. You can keep playing them infinitely, and they're all ups and downs and ups and downs. And I guarantee you, every time you get money, you'll be afraid you're going to lose it, or you're going to compare it to the next person who has it. Or every time you get famous, now that's, let's say that's the image that you have to live up to, and now insults can hurt you, and now you don't have your privacy anymore, as you've written about. So all of these games have downsides. Or if you're, you know, very, you're good at dating, you have a lot of men or women in your life, then you're also going to have a lot of relationship trauma and you've got a lot of tumultuousness, you know, a lot of hurt people, you got a lot of emotional drama in your life. So you just have to kind of realize when you won the game and say I'm done with this. So I would aspire to be like Socrates and say there are so many things in this world that I do not want. And I like to think that I'm kind of there for the most part. Either I don't want it or I have it now. Of course, there's a fear of losing it which is always there, but I don't want to pick up new desires unconsciously, and I don't want to keep upgrading my lifestyle and my expectations to match my circumstances, otherwise I'll be on this treadmill forever. We are all playing games, and the key is knowing which game you are playing so you can at least attempt to consciously choose the game. Finite and Infinite Games by James Carse is an interesting read. And I just want to close with, and then you're certainly more than welcome to add any closing comments, but a quote that really makes me think of you. It's one of my favorite quotes from Feynman. So Richard P. Feynman, once again, here's the quote: "The first principle is that you must not fool yourself, and you are the easiest person to fool." I love that quote. I love that quote. I read it when I was 9 or 10 years old and I've never forgotten it. It is so important. And none of us are perfect. We all have flaws, we all have weaknesses, we all have vices. Or I'll speak for myself, certainly that's true of me. But Naval, as much as anyone else, perhaps more than anyone else I know, have gone to great lengths to become self-aware. Furthermore, and this is I think that one of the distinguishing characteristics for me that you represent, becoming self-aware and spending time on the timeless while also reserving the right with competency to be a very high level operator in the real world. Right? Because you can be not of this world by opting out completely and go into a world of introspection, but in truth lack the resilience and emotional awareness to interact with any type of real stressor, which doesn't appeal to me certainly. And you can be so engulfed by being in the world that you lose self-awareness, you stop realizing that you are playing certain games, you become addicted and self-identified with things that come from sort of consensus reality. That's also not appealing. But you have managed, in a way that I find fascinating, to combine the cultivation of self-awareness with operating in the world at a high level. I wouldn't say I'm necessarily there yet, but it gets a little easier every year.
turns.zh: 发现者说了这么多我不想要的东西，我觉得那就是自由，那就是力量，那就是自足，那是一个找到了自我、不需要外在任何东西的人。这太鼓舞人心了。所以同样地，我看着这个世界说，嗯，我也可以这么说，对吧？你不能就这么说。如果我只是说有很多我不想要的东西，那不是真的。我想要钱，我想要女孩，你知道，我想要名声，否则我就不会上播客，也不会在推特上。在某种程度上这是真的。所以显然有些东西是我想要的。我不是和尚，我没住在道场。但我能做到的是，我已经得到了我想要的东西，而且我小心不去想要更多。我不想要更多名声，我不想要更多钱。或者如果我得到了，那也没关系，那是手艺的一部分，是额外的奖励，你知道。所以我必须小心不要无意识地接受新的欲望。如果我有了新的欲望，我最好去实现它。所以这些都只是我在玩的游戏，你知道，你必须玩一些游戏。你在这个星球上，你活着，你不如玩点什么。现在问题是你玩什么游戏，以及如何退出游戏，这样你就不会永远被困在玩那个游戏里。一种方法是谨慎选择你的游戏。如果你是和尚，你只玩很少的游戏，或者你不玩游戏，你过着满足、幸福、和谐、平静的生活。另一种是你玩游戏并赢得它，然后你说我现在自由了。所以那条推文，那条推文就是玩游戏的理由。所以赢得游戏的理由是为了摆脱它。这是提醒我自己，对于我已经赢得的游戏，是时候放手了，摆脱它们，而不是无意识地通过和别人攀比来加倍下注，继续升级再升级再升级再升级，然后有一天你死了，然后你会想，好吧，那毫无意义。就像，你知道，我做了所有那些工作为了什么？为了什么？所以如果你不再享受它，如果你已经按照你开始玩游戏时的定义赢得了游戏，一个技巧是尽早设定定义，这样当你超过它时你就知道自己赢了，以免永远被困在玩这个游戏里，只是生活在焦虑的未来中，而不是真正享受当下。你必须知道什么时候停止玩游戏。所以对我来说，玩这些游戏的理由是赢得它们，然后你就可以摆脱它们，看看你还想做什么或不想做什么，而不是永远玩同一个游戏。因为这些游戏，成年人的游戏，设计得非常巧妙。你可以无限地玩下去，它们都是起起落落起起落落。我向你保证，每次你得到钱，你会害怕失去它，或者你会和下一个拥有它的人比较。或者每次你出名了，那就是你必须维护的形象，现在侮辱可以伤害你，现在你不再有隐私，就像你写过的。所以所有这些游戏都有缺点。或者如果你，你知道，很擅长约会，你的生活中有很多男人或女人，那么你也会有很多关系创伤，有很多动荡，你知道，很多受伤的人，你的生活中有很多情感戏剧。所以你只需要意识到你什么时候赢了游戏，然后说我已经完成了这个。所以我会向往像苏格拉底一样，说这个世界上有很多我不想要的东西。而且我倾向于认为我大部分时候已经达到了。要么我不想要它，要么我现在已经拥有了。当然，总是有失去它的恐惧，但我不想无意识地接受新的欲望，我不想不断升级我的生活方式和期望来匹配我的环境，否则我会永远在这个跑步机上。我们都在玩游戏，关键是知道你在玩哪个游戏，这样你至少可以尝试有意识地选择游戏。詹姆斯·卡斯写的《有限与无限的游戏》是一本有趣的读物。我想用一句话来结束，然后你当然欢迎添加任何结束语，但有一句让我想起你的名言。这是我最喜欢的费曼名言之一。所以理查德·P·费曼，再一次，这是名言：“首要原则是你不能欺骗自己，而你是最容易欺骗的人。”我喜欢那句话。我喜欢那句话。我九岁或十岁时读到它，从未忘记。它非常重要。我们都不完美。我们都有缺点，我们都有弱点，我们都有恶习。或者我为自己说话，我当然是这样。但纳瓦尔，和任何人一样，也许比我认识的任何人都更努力地变得自我觉察。此外，我认为这是你代表的一个显著特征，变得自我觉察并花时间在永恒的事物上，同时保留能力在现实世界中成为非常高水平的操作者。对吧？因为你可以完全退出这个世界，进入内省的世界，但实际上缺乏与任何真实压力源互动的韧性和情感意识，这当然不吸引我。你也可以如此沉浸于世界而失去自我觉察，你不再意识到你在玩某些游戏，你变得上瘾并与来自共识现实的事物自我认同。那也不吸引人。但你以一种我觉得迷人的方式，将自我觉察的培养与在世界上高水平运作结合起来。我不会说我一定已经达到了，但每年都变得容易一点。

turns.en: The Feynman quote is brilliant. He's right. We fool ourselves all the time. And I still fool myself all the time because I still have strong beliefs and opinions. For example, even though I don't tweet a lot about politics, I do have strong political opinions. And that is a form of fooling myself because, you know, politics is a man-made game. It doesn't really exist in the real world. No tree is aware of politics. And I do, even though I don't like to, even some of my beliefs are filtered and lined up in bundles based on my chosen media sources and whatnot. So I fool myself all the time. But if you can fool yourself a little bit less, then you can navigate reality much better. It doesn't take a lot. Most people are just living very unconsciously, you know, an unexamined life. So it just gives you kind of a leg up. And I agree with you in that you can always check out of the game and it's easier that way, but then you're fragile, right? You kind of build resilience by working out hard. And it's hard to be calm until you've lost a lot and won a lot. Like you have to see.
turns.zh: 费曼的名言很精彩。他说得对。我们一直在欺骗自己。我仍然一直在欺骗自己，因为我仍然有强烈的信念和观点。例如，即使我不怎么在推特上谈论政治，我确实有强烈的政治观点。那是一种欺骗自己的形式，因为，你知道，政治是人造的游戏。它在现实世界中并不真正存在。没有树知道政治。而我确实，即使我不喜欢，我的一些信念也是基于我选择的媒体来源等过滤和捆绑在一起的。所以我一直在欺骗自己。但如果你能少欺骗自己一点，那么你就能更好地驾驭现实。这不需要太多。大多数人只是非常无意识地生活，你知道，未经审视的生活。所以这给了你一种优势。我同意你的观点，你可以随时退出游戏，那样更容易，但那样你很脆弱，对吧？你通过努力锻炼来建立韧性。而且很难保持平静，直到你失去很多并赢得很多。就像你必须看到。

turns.en: The ups and the downs and the ups and the downs before you get tired of the dopamine chase and you sort of realize like okay this is down but eventually there'll be an up or there's an up that'll be a down I know how this game works. So one of the things I've been tracking recently is I've been trying to make a list of what are the activities that are sort of non-dual in nature they don't create their own opposite down the road. So chasing money does create anxiety and suffering down the road as you compare and you win and lose and so on. Chasing fame also has downsides where you're not open to insult and attacks and being canceled and whatnot. So what activities are intrinsically true in and of themselves and are kind of a free lunch? And those include things like meditation, yoga, creating art, playing, reading for fun not necessarily for knowledge or education, writing, journaling. You can even be building a business as long as you're doing it for the craft of it, for the flow of it, not necessarily for a given outcome. Although it's hard to do that. At least externally it seems like Elon Musk is doing that, he's building rockets to get to Mars, not to make money or to be famous, although he's getting those byproducts. So I think this whole not fooling yourself, paying attention to yourself, not taking yourself too seriously, examining your own thoughts from first principles, and doing activities that are drawn intrinsically for you as opposed to for the external world, they just make your life better. I'm not doing these things to be a Stoic sage, right? Honestly, one of the downsides of the Stoics is they don't look like they're having a lot of fun. I still like that right now, obviously a lot has been lost in translation. I know they had their vomitoriums and their orgies and whatnot, but the Stoics, at least the way it's modernly projected, and this is one of the reasons why I do like Osho. People give Osho a lot of flack and they should, he was a little crazy in some ways, but he was having fun, he had a good time. And you're alive, you have one very short life. When your life ends, it goes to zero. To you, it's indistinguishable from your perspective. Your death is indistinguishable from the end of the world as far as you're concerned. The world has disappeared because when you came into existence the world appeared, when you go out of existence the world disappears. And that is so consequential that it makes the rest of your life inconsequential, and that is a form of freedom. And so you should enjoy yourself, you should not suffer in this life. If you are just constantly suffering, then figure out how to get out of that suffering. If you can't get out of that suffering, then figure out how to redefine your internal worldview so that you don't necessarily view it as suffering anymore, to the extent that you need to get out of it. These days we borrow a lot of suffering on behalf of other people. A lot of people want to be a little Christ carrying crosses for others. It's a miserable existence. You're actually not doing them any favors either. You carrying their cross isn't making their life any better. The better way is for you to be happy and successful and healthy and then go save them, like literally save them physically, not necessarily just sit around suffering for them. Or at least that's the life that I choose to live.
turns.zh: 在追逐多巴胺的过程中，你会经历起起落落，直到厌倦，然后意识到：好吧，现在是低谷，但最终会有高峰；或者有高峰就会伴随低谷——我懂这个游戏规则了。所以我最近一直在做的一件事，就是列一个清单，找出那些本质上非二元、不会在未来制造对立面的活动。追逐金钱确实会在未来带来焦虑和痛苦，因为你会比较、会赢也会输。追逐名声也有坏处，你会对侮辱、攻击和封杀变得敏感。那么，哪些活动本身是内在真实的，是一种免费的午餐呢？这些包括冥想、瑜伽、创作艺术、玩耍、为乐趣而阅读（不一定为了知识或教育）、写作、记日记。你甚至可以创业，只要你是为了手艺本身、为了心流，而不是为了某个特定结果。虽然这很难做到。至少从外部看，埃隆·马斯克似乎就是这样，他造火箭是为了去火星，不是为了赚钱或出名，尽管他得到了这些副产品。所以我认为，不欺骗自己、关注自己、不把自己太当回事、从第一性原理审视自己的思想、以及从事那些内在驱动而非外部驱动的活动，这些都能让你的生活变得更好。我做这些不是为了成为一个斯多葛派的圣人。老实说，斯多葛派的一个缺点是，他们看起来并不怎么快乐。当然，很多内容在翻译中丢失了。我知道他们有呕吐室和狂欢派对什么的，但斯多葛派至少在现代的投射中是这样的，这也是我喜欢奥修的原因之一。人们经常批评奥修，他们也该批评，他在某些方面有点疯狂，但他过得很快乐，他享受生活。你活着，只有一次短暂的生命。当生命结束时，一切归零。对你来说，从你的视角看，你的死亡和世界末日没有区别。世界消失了，因为当你出现时世界出现，当你消失时世界消失。这个事实如此重大，以至于让你生命的其余部分变得无关紧要，而这本身就是一种自由。所以你应该享受自己，不应该在生命中受苦。如果你一直在受苦，那就想办法摆脱痛苦。如果你无法摆脱，那就想办法重新定义你的内心世界观，让你不再把它视为痛苦，至少到足以摆脱它的程度。如今，我们经常为他人背负痛苦。很多人想成为小基督，为他人背负十字架。这是一种悲惨的存在。你实际上也没有帮到他们。你背负他们的十字架并不会让他们的生活变得更好。更好的方式是让自己快乐、成功、健康，然后去拯救他们，真正地拯救他们，而不是仅仅坐在那里为他们受苦。至少这是我选择的生活方式。

ts.sec: The story of Craig and the decision to be happy

turns.en: I'm going to end with one story. There was a guy that I met in Thailand, Craig, I forget his last name, but he used to work for Tony Robbins. I met this guy and we were on vacation in Thailand and he was just the happiest person. He was just happy all the time and he just seemed to be able to laugh it off. As far as he had a good life, he had stuff going for him, but it wasn't like he had this incredibly enviable life where you would drop everything and move to Thailand and adopt his lifestyle. He had an okay life, but he was just happy, and not in a forced way, but he was genuinely happy. So I asked him, I said, you know, what's your secret? Why? And he said that he used to work for Tony Robbins and used to fly around setting up events for Tony, and he was always sitting in the front row and taking notes and setting things up and learning and listening. And he just kind of started seeing through his own internal BS. He got to this point where he was fooling himself into unhappiness, and he just decided one day he said, you know what, I have no responsibility to anybody in this world other than me. Or if I do have a responsibility, this kind of weight of responsibility that goes on where we're like, oh I need to live up to some imagined self, what he realized was he said, somebody in this world has to be happy all the time. That is somebody's job to be that role model for people in the world. That might as well be me. I'll be that guy. I'll take on that burden. And he decided to be the happy one. And I just thought that was simple and brilliant, and he did it. He lived there. So I want to be that guy who is successful, peaceful, happy, enjoying life, blissful, meditative, spiritual, successful, and as healthy as I can be, and famous and rich, and not give a damn about any of it. So if I lose it all tomorrow, I still want to be happy. That's it. That's where I want to be. And I'll just make that decision because somebody has to be that person. It might as well be me.
turns.zh: 我想用一个故事来结尾。我在泰国遇到一个人，叫克雷格，我忘了他姓什么，但他曾为托尼·罗宾斯工作。我们当时在泰国度假，他是最快乐的人。他一直都很开心，似乎总能一笑而过。他的生活不错，有一些成就，但并不是那种让人羡慕到要放弃一切搬到泰国去过他那种生活的程度。他的生活还算可以，但他就是很快乐，不是装出来的，而是发自内心的快乐。所以我问他，你的秘诀是什么？为什么？他说，他以前为托尼·罗宾斯工作，到处飞着为托尼布置活动，他总是坐在第一排，记笔记，布置现场，学习和倾听。然后他开始看穿自己内心的那些废话。他到了那个点，意识到自己一直在欺骗自己，让自己不快乐。有一天他决定，你知道吗，我对这个世界上的任何人都没有责任，除了我自己。或者说，如果我有责任，那种让我们觉得必须活成某个想象中的自己的沉重责任，他意识到的是：这个世界上必须有人一直快乐。那是某人的工作，成为世界的榜样。那为什么不能是我呢？我来当那个人。我来承担那个负担。于是他决定成为那个快乐的人。我觉得这简单又 brilliant，而且他真的做到了。他住在那里。所以我想成为那样的人：成功、平和、快乐、享受生活、幸福、冥想、灵性、成功，尽可能健康，有名有钱，但毫不在意这些。如果我明天失去一切，我仍然想快乐。就是这样。那就是我想达到的状态。我就做这个决定，因为必须有人成为那个人。那为什么不能是我呢？

turns.en: I love it, man. Naval Ravikant, otherwise known as Naval Ravikant, but Naval on Twitter, N-A-V-A-L. And he has the podcast, of course, Naval, N-A-V-A-L, you can find that anywhere you find your podcasts. The blog nav.al. Previous conversations we've had, and we've had quite a few, you can just search Naval on the website tim.blog, forward slash Naval. Anything else you would like to add? Any other resources, places people can say hello on the internet, recommendations, asks of the audience?
turns.zh: 太棒了，老兄。Naval Ravikant，也就是 Naval Ravikant，Twitter 上是 Naval，N-A-V-A-L。他当然也有播客，就叫 Naval，N-A-V-A-L，你可以在任何播客平台上找到。博客是 nav.al。我们之前有过不少对话，你可以在网站 tim.blog 上搜索 Naval 找到。还有什么想补充的吗？其他资源、人们在网上打招呼的地方、推荐、对听众的请求？

turns.en: I'm mostly on Twitter. Eric Jorgensen recently put out a book which had a bunch of my sayings called 'The Almanack of Naval Ravikant'. It was very nice of him. I don't make any money off of it. It's free on the internet, you can just download it. I think it's 'Navalmanack', clever little turn of phrase. I think that's it. We'll see you on the interwebs. I wish everybody has a wealthy, healthy, and happy life. Go get it.
turns.zh: 我主要在 Twitter 上。Eric Jorgensen 最近出了一本书，收录了我很多语录，叫《Naval Ravikant 年鉴》。他真是太好了。我没从中赚一分钱。它在网上是免费的，你可以直接下载。我觉得叫 'Navalmanack'，挺巧妙的双关。就这些吧。我们网上见。祝大家富有、健康、快乐。去争取吧。

turns.en: Thanks so much, Naval, for taking the time. This is awesome. And to everybody listening, thank you for tuning in. And until next time, don't fool yourself. Sit down, spend some time with your mind, and don't be too hard on that roommate in your head. Hey guys, this is Tim again. Just a few more things before you take off. Number one, this is Five Bullet Friday. Do you want to get a short email from me? Would you enjoy getting a short email from me every Friday that provides...
turns.zh: 非常感谢 Naval 抽出时间。这太棒了。也感谢所有听众的收听。下次再见，不要欺骗自己。坐下来，花点时间与你的内心相处，不要对你脑海中的那个室友太苛刻。嘿，大家好，我是 Tim。在你们离开之前还有几件事。第一，这是 Five Bullet Friday。你想收到我的简短邮件吗？你愿意每周五收到我的一封简短邮件，提供……

consensus.en: Wealth requires owning equity in a business, not renting out your time.
consensus.zh: 财富需要拥有企业股权，而不是出租你的时间。

consensus.en: Long-term thinking is the foundation of all effective self-help and success.
consensus.zh: 长期思维是所有有效自助和成功的基础。

consensus.en: The most important relationship is with yourself; self-examination reduces anxiety.
consensus.zh: 最重要的关系是与自己的关系；自我审视能减少焦虑。

consensus.en: Cryptocurrencies enable sovereign-resistant wealth storage and transfer without intermediaries.
consensus.zh: 加密货币实现了无需中介的主权抵抗型财富存储和转移。

consensus.en: Understanding first principles beats memorizing jargon; true knowledge is rederivable.
consensus.zh: 理解第一原理胜过记忆术语；真正的知识是可以重新推导的。

consensus.en: Choose long-term games with long-term people for compound returns in all areas.
consensus.zh: 选择长期游戏和长期伙伴，在所有领域获得复利回报。

contrarian.en: Science is not consensus or voting; it requires falsifiability and risky predictions.
contrarian.zh: 科学不是共识或投票；它需要可证伪性和风险预测。

contrarian.en: Stable coins have hidden costs: fraud risk, censorship risk, or blow-up risk.
contrarian.zh: 稳定币有隐藏成本：欺诈风险、审查风险或崩盘风险。

contrarian.en: The best relationships don't feel like work; effortless chemistry beats effort.
contrarian.zh: 最好的关系不觉得是工作；毫不费力的化学反应胜过刻意努力。

contrarian.en: The reason to win the game is to be free of it, not to keep playing.
contrarian.zh: 赢得游戏的理由是为了摆脱它，而不是继续玩下去。

contrarian.en: Calmness is a superpower; anxiety is not necessary for effectiveness.
contrarian.zh: 冷静是一种超能力；焦虑并非高效的必要条件。

contrarian.en: Meditation should ruin your current life; it leads to real change, not comfort.
contrarian.zh: 冥想应该摧毁你当前的生活；它带来真正的改变，而非舒适。
